title: 🏷️ v97.5.0 — Deterministic Law Promotion Engine (Hardened & Verified)
body:
🏷️ v97.5.0 — Deterministic Law Promotion Engine (Hardened & Verified)
📦 Overview

Introduces a fully deterministic Law Promotion Engine that converts validated conjectures into minimal, reproducible, and domain-safe laws.

This release includes critical hardening to ensure that promoted laws never generalize beyond validated data, establishing a reliable foundation for law evolution, experiment strategy, and decoder synthesis.

✨ Core Features
🧠 Deterministic Law Promotion
Conjectures are promoted into structured Law objects with:
explicit Condition operators (gt, gte, lt, lte, eq, neq)
AND-based condition evaluation
deterministic SHA-256 law IDs
Fully reproducible: identical inputs → identical laws
🔬 Safe Condition Minimization
minimize_conditions now enforces domain invariance:
compares matching-run index sets before and after removal
rejects any condition removal that expands into unvalidated regions
Prevents over-generalization from sparse data

Guarantees:

law domain == validated domain
🌐 Empty-Condition Laws (Global Rules)
Laws with zero conditions are now allowed only if:
consistency == 1.0
no domain expansion occurs

Enables discovery of true global invariants:

IF (always) → action
🛡️ Deterministic Conjecture Validation
_validate_conjecture() ensures:
correct structure (dict, required keys)
valid condition schema (metric, operator, value)
operator validity
Invalid inputs are safely rejected (None), never raising exceptions
📚 Law Registry (Conflict-Safe & Deduplicated)
LawRegistry supports:
conflict detection via overlapping condition domains
deterministic resolution:
higher law_score
higher specificity (more conditions)
lexicographic ordering
Deduplication added:
identical law IDs are rejected before insertion
prevents duplicate rules and downstream double-application
📊 Scoring System

Each law includes:

consistency (must equal 1.0)
coverage (fraction of runs where conditions hold)
simplicity = 1 / (1 + n_conditions)
law_score = coverage * simplicity

Promoted only if:

consistency == 1.0 AND law_score ≥ threshold
🧪 Tests
50 tests passing
Includes:
Core Behavior
condition evaluation (all operators)
scoring correctness (coverage, simplicity, law_score)
minimization correctness
Hardening Coverage
domain expansion prevention (critical safety test)
empty-condition law reduction
malformed conjecture rejection (5 edge cases)
duplicate law prevention
multi-conflict replacement (removes all conflicting laws)
Determinism
identical inputs → identical outputs across runs
🧠 Design Guarantees

This release ensures:

- no over-generalization
- no duplicate laws
- no crashes on malformed input
- no nondeterministic behavior
- no hidden domain expansion

title: v97.4.0 — Curvature Dynamics (Second-Order Geometry Extension)
body:
📦 Release Notes

Introduces a second-order curvature metric to the correction dynamics pipeline, enabling geometric analysis of trajectory behavior.

Added
compute_curvature(projection_distances)
Implements discrete second-difference operator: d[i+1] - 2d[i] + d[i-1]
Outputs:
mean_curvature — direction of trajectory bending
abs_curvature — magnitude of curvature
curvature_variation — stability of curvature over time
Handles edge cases (len < 3) deterministically
Integration
compute_friction_score
Adds curvature to extended_components
Core friction calculation unchanged
classify_dynamics
Adds optional labels:
+high_curvature (abs_curvature > 0.3)
+basin_entry (mean_curvature < 0)
+resistant (mean_curvature > 0)
Fully backward compatible
print_dynamics_report
Displays curvature metrics (mean, abs, variation)
Tests
+20 new tests (111 total passing)
Coverage includes:
constant, linear, accelerating, oscillatory trajectories
classification behavior
pipeline integration
determinism and no-mutation guarantees
Design Notes
Deterministic and additive
No changes to existing metrics or outputs
Extends dynamics space with second-order geometry
Completes the trajectory analysis stack: distance → acceleration → curvature

title: v97.3.0 — Advanced Dynamics Extensions (Twist, Nonlocal Influence, Acceleration)
body:
📦 Release Notes

Adds higher-order deterministic dynamics metrics to the correction analysis pipeline.

Added
compute_loop_twist
Detects orientation-flipping loops (A → B → A with representation drift)
Outputs: twist_count, twist_ratio
compute_nonlocal_influence
Detects indirect state changes without local input change
Outputs: nonlocal_events, nonlocal_ratio
compute_acceleration
Measures convergence speed via mean absolute distance deltas
Outputs: mean_delta, acceleration_score
Integration
compute_friction_score
Now returns extended_components (twist, nonlocal, acceleration)
Core friction formula unchanged
classify_dynamics
Supports extended labels:
+twisted
+nonlocal
+accelerated
Fully backward compatible
run_correction_dynamics
Propagates extended metrics through pipeline
print_dynamics_report
Displays extended dynamics block
Tests
+22 new tests (91 total passing)
Coverage includes:
twist detection (loop vs non-loop)
nonlocal influence (indirect vs local)
acceleration behavior (increasing vs constant)
determinism + no mutation guarantees
integration correctness
Design Notes
Fully deterministic (no randomness)
Additive only (no schema or behavior changes)
Maintains strict layer separation (analysis only)
Extends dynamics space: geometry, causality, convergence

title: v97.2.0 — Conjecture Execution & Validation Engine
body:
## [v97.2.0] — Conjecture Execution & Validation Engine

Introduces a deterministic experiment layer that executes and validates
system-generated conjectures.

This release completes the hypothesis–test loop by enabling automatic
evaluation of conjectures against observed system behavior.

### Added

- Conjecture execution module (src/qec/analysis/conjecture_execution.py):

  - `normalize_conjecture_inputs()`:
    combines conjectures (v97.1) with system metrics

  - `execute_conjecture()`:
    maps conjecture types to measurable system metrics:
      - oscillation_ratio
      - churn_score
      - friction_score
      - stability_efficiency

  - `validate_conjecture()`:
    compares expected vs observed outcomes:
      - confirmed
      - rejected
      - inconclusive

  - `score_validation()`:
    deterministic scoring:
      +2 strong confirmation
      +1 weak confirmation
      0 inconclusive
      -1 rejected

  - `run_conjecture_execution()`:
    full pipeline entry point

  - `print_conjecture_results()`:
    human-readable output with status labels

### Testing

- Added conjecture execution test suite (44 tests):
  - input normalization
  - metric mapping correctness
  - validation logic
  - scoring rules
  - pipeline integration
  - print formatting
  - determinism and no-mutation guarantees

### Key Outcomes

- Enables automatic execution of conjecture tests
- Provides deterministic validation of hypotheses
- Introduces structured experiment results
- Bridges hypothesis generation and empirical evaluation

### System Impact

This release completes the scientific loop:

laws → reasoning → conjectures → experiments → validation

It establishes a deterministic framework for hypothesis testing and
evidence-based evaluation.

All changes are additive, deterministic, and fully inspectable.

title: v97.0.0 — Law-Guided Control Fusion
body:
## [v97.0.0] — Law-Guided Control Fusion

Introduces a deterministic inference layer that integrates extracted laws
into correction strategy selection.

This release upgrades the system from friction-aware control to
law-guided decision-making.

### Added

- Law-guided control module (src/qec/analysis/law_control.py):

  - `match_laws()`:
    matches candidate records against rulebook laws

  - `compute_law_score()`:
    discrete scoring:
      +2 strong match
      +1 moderate match
      -1 contradiction

  - `apply_law_prior()`:
    integrates law score into decision:
      effective_score = efficiency + (law_score * 0.1)

  - `select_with_laws()`:
    deterministic ranking:
      effective_score → stability → compression → friction → mode

  - `run_law_control()`:
    full pipeline combining:
      - friction-aware candidates (v96.4)
      - rulebook laws (v96.2)

  - `print_law_control()`:
    human-readable decision output

### Testing

- Added law control test suite (40 tests):
  - law matching correctness
  - scoring logic
  - law prior application
  - deterministic ranking and tie-breaking
  - pipeline integration
  - print formatting
  - no-mutation guarantees

### Key Outcomes

- Enables law-guided decision-making
- Integrates knowledge base into control loop
- Maintains deterministic, interpretable reasoning
- Provides traceable decision rationale

### System Impact

This release upgrades the system from control to reasoning:

measure → analyze → extract laws → evaluate dynamics → decide → reason → decide

It establishes a deterministic inference engine for correction strategies.

All changes are additive, deterministic, and fully inspectable.

title: v96.4.0 — Friction-Aware Correction Control
body:
## [v96.4.0] — Friction-Aware Correction Control

Introduces a deterministic control layer that selects correction
strategies based on both performance and dynamic efficiency.

This release completes the decision loop by integrating friction-aware
analysis into mode selection.

### Added

- Friction-aware control module (src/qec/analysis/friction_control.py):

  - `normalize_control_inputs()`:
    combines:
      - hierarchical correction (v96.0)
      - invariant analysis (v95.2)
      - law extraction (v96.2)
      - correction dynamics (v96.3)

  - `compute_efficiency()`:
    efficiency metric:
      stability_efficiency / (1 + friction_score)

  - `rank_control_strategies()`:
    deterministic ranking:
      efficiency → stability → compression → friction → name

  - Friction-aware rules:
    - high friction override
    - oscillation suppression
    - churn rejection
    - conflict avoidance

  - `compute_control_decision()`:
    hybrid control combining ranking and rule-based adjustments

  - `run_friction_control()`:
    full pipeline entry point

  - `print_control_report()`:
    structured, human-readable decision output

### Testing

- Added friction control test suite (59 tests):
  - efficiency calculation
  - ranking correctness
  - rule application and overrides
  - decision stability
  - pipeline integration
  - determinism and no-mutation guarantees

### Key Outcomes

- Enables selection of low-dissipation correction strategies
- Integrates dynamic efficiency into decision-making

### System Impact

measure → analyze → extract laws → evaluate dynamics → decide

framework.

title: v96.3.0 — Correction Hysteresis & Friction Analysis
body:
## [v96.3.0] — Correction Hysteresis & Friction Analysis

Introduces a deterministic dynamics layer that measures internal
correction inefficiency via hysteresis, oscillation, switching instability,
projection churn, and invariant conflict dissipation.

This release adds a physics-inspired “friction” model for correction behavior.

### Added

- Correction dynamics module (src/qec/analysis/correction_dynamics.py):

  - `extract_correction_traces()`:
    normalizes correction outputs into per-system traces

  - `detect_oscillation()`:
    detects A → B → A correction loops

  - `detect_hysteresis()`:
    identifies path-dependent correction outcomes

  - `compute_mode_switches()`:
    measures frequency of mode transitions

  - `switching_instability_score()`:
    detects repeated back-and-forth switching patterns

  - `compute_projection_churn()`:
    quantifies wasted correction effort:
      total_projection × (1 - stability_efficiency)

  - `invariant_conflict_dissipation()`:
    penalizes conflicting invariant interactions

  - `compute_friction_score()`:
    additive combination of all components

  - `classify_dynamics()`:
    labels regimes:
      - stable
      - adaptive
      - frictional

  - `run_correction_dynamics()`:
    full pipeline entry point

  - `print_dynamics_report()`:
    structured text output

### Testing

- Added correction dynamics test suite (55 tests):
  - oscillation detection
  - hysteresis detection
  - switching metrics
  - projection churn calculation
  - conflict dissipation
  - friction score composition
  - classification thresholds
  - determinism and no-mutation guarantees

### Key Outcomes

- Enables measurement of internal correction inefficiency
- Introduces hysteresis and oscillation detection
- Quantifies energy-like dissipation in correction processes
- Adds regime classification for system dynamics

### System Impact

This release upgrades the system from structural analysis to
dynamic efficiency analysis:

structure → theory → dynamics

It establishes a deterministic framework for measuring how efficiently
correction processes converge.

All changes are additive, deterministic, and fully inspectable.

title: v96.1.0 — Deterministic Sonification of Hierarchical Correction & Invariant Dynamics
body:
## [v96.1.0] — Deterministic Sonification of Hierarchical Correction & Invariant Dynamics

Introduces a deterministic sonification layer that converts hierarchical

    invariant types mapped to harmonic frequency ratios

  - `sonify_stages()`:
    sequential audio construction of hierarchical correction stages

title: v96.0.0 — Hierarchical Correction & Core Invariant Promotion

  - `compare_hierarchical_modes()`:

- Core invariant module (src/qec/analysis/core_invariants.py):

  - `apply_core_invariant_overlay()`:

  - `evaluate_core_overlay_with_hierarchy()`:

invariant → promotion → hierarchical geometry → unified evaluation

capable of generating, validating, and organizing correction strategies.

title: v95.1.0 — Invariant Application & Overlay-Based Evaluation
body:
## [v95.1.0] — Invariant Application & Overlay-Based Evaluation

Introduces an invariant application layer that applies validated
invariants as overlay constraints and evaluates their real impact on
system behavior.

This release completes the invariant lifecycle by moving from synthesis
and validation to application and verification.

### Added

- Invariant application module (src/qec/analysis/invariant_application.py):

  - `get_accepted_invariants()`:
    extracts accepted invariants from synthesis reports

  - Overlay-based invariant application:
    - local_stability_constraint:
      filters low-stability modes
    - equivalence_class_constraint:
      prioritizes high-compression modes
    - geometry_alignment_constraint:
      enforces projection alignment (square / d4)
    - explicit_allowed_state_constraint:
      prioritizes invariant-guided modes
    - bounded_projection_constraint:
      removes overcorrection regimes

  - `evaluate_overlay()`:
    compares system behavior before and after invariant application using:
      stability_efficiency → compression_efficiency → stability_gain

  - `run_invariant_application()`:
    full pipeline supporting raw data or precomputed synthesis reports

  - `print_application_report()`:
    deterministic, human-readable output

### Design Constraints

- No mutation of base system data
- No modification of correction engine
- Overlay-only application model
- Deterministic filtering and prioritization
- Consistent evaluation metric hierarchy

### Testing

- Added invariant application test suite (51 tests):
  - overlay correctness for all invariant types
  - evaluation logic validation
  - pipeline integration
  - determinism and no-mutation guarantees
  - edge case handling

- Total tests: 100+ (all passing)

### Key Outcomes

- Enables application of validated invariants without altering core system
- Provides deterministic verification of invariant impact
- Completes invariant lifecycle: synthesis → validation → application → verification
- Maintains full interpretability and reproducibility

### System Impact

This release upgrades the system from invariant validation to invariant application:

gap → invariant → validation → application → verification

It establishes a closed-loop structural evolution pipeline and prepares the system for:

- invariant interaction analysis (v95.2)
- multi-level correction strategies
- deeper integration of structure, geometry, and control

All changes are additive, deterministic, and fully inspectable.

title: v94.0.0 — Controlled Self-Adjustment & Rollback-Safe Policy Simulation
body:
## [v94.0.0] — Controlled Self-Adjustment & Rollback-Safe Policy Simulation

Introduces a deterministic self-adjustment layer that evaluates
recommendations by simulating policy changes and accepting them only
when they produce measurable improvements.

This release marks the transition from recommendation to controlled,
rollback-safe system adaptation.

### Added

- Self-adjustment module (src/qec/analysis/self_adjustment.py):
  - `_RECOMMENDATION_TO_MODE`:
    deterministic mapping from recommendations to candidate modes
  - `resolve_recommendation_to_mode()`:
    resolves candidate modes with explicit fallback chains
  - `is_improvement()`:
    strict deterministic comparison:
      stability_efficiency → compression_efficiency → stability_gain
  - `adjust_system()`:
    applies a single recommendation and evaluates accept/reject outcome
  - `run_self_adjustment()`:
    full adjustment pipeline supporting:
      - raw benchmark results
      - summary output
      - diagnostics output
  - `print_adjustment_report()`:
    sorted, text-based adjustment report

### Design Constraints

- Only one recommendation applied per system
- No re-execution of correction engine
- No parameter tuning or search
- All decisions are deterministic and inspectable
- Explicit rollback via rejection

### Testing

- Added comprehensive adjustment test suite (50 tests):
  - recommendation-to-mode resolution and fallback chains
  - improvement criterion validation
  - acceptance and rejection scenarios
  - input flexibility across pipeline stages
  - deterministic output guarantees
  - no mutation of inputs
  - print layer validation

- Total tests: 174 (all passing)

### Key Outcomes

- Enables deterministic evaluation of recommended changes
- Introduces rollback-safe decision logic
- Bridges diagnostics and future adaptive behavior
- Establishes policy simulation framework

### System Impact

This release upgrades the system from recommendation to controlled adaptation:

diagnostics → recommendation → simulated adjustment → accept/reject

It forms the foundation for:

- structural gap detection (v94.1)
- invariant opportunity discovery
- constraint synthesis and validation (v95)

All changes are additive, deterministic, and fully inspectable.

title: v93.1.0 — Taxonomy-Aware Diagnostics & System Classification
body:
## [v93.1.0] — Taxonomy-Aware Diagnostics & System Classification

Extends the deterministic self-diagnostics layer with system classification
and taxonomy-aware reasoning, enabling context-sensitive issue detection
and recommendations based on DFA behavioral patterns.

### Added

- System classification layer:
  - `infer_system_class()`:
    deterministic rule-based classifier producing:
      - chain_like
      - cycle_like
      - branching_like
      - basin_like
      - degenerate
      - unknown
  - `classify_all_systems()`:
    applies classification across all benchmarked systems

- Metric extraction helper:
  - `get_mode_metrics()`:
    explicit retrieval of per-mode metrics for classification

### Enhanced

- Taxonomy-aware issue detection:
  - cycle_mismatch
  - chain_underperformance
  - missing_invariant_guidance
  - overprocessing_simple_system

- Extended recommendation mapping:
  - prefer_invariant_guided_modes
  - prefer_d4_projection
  - enable_invariant_guidance
  - disable_correction

- Diagnostics pipeline (`run_self_diagnostics()`):
  - now includes `system_classes`
  - merges taxonomy-aware issues with base issue set

- Diagnostics output (`print_diagnostics()`):
  - displays inferred system class per DFA

### Testing

- Added taxonomy diagnostics test suite (50 tests):
  - classification across all system types
  - determinism and no-mutation guarantees
  - taxonomy issue triggering
  - recommendation mapping validation
  - full pipeline integration

- Total tests: 81 (all passing)

### Key Outcomes

- Enables classification of system behavior patterns across DFA types
- Introduces context-aware diagnostics and recommendations
- Bridges benchmark results with structural interpretation
- Prepares system for policy-based correction and self-adjustment

### System Impact

This release upgrades diagnostics from generic rule evaluation to
taxonomy-aware reasoning:

metrics → classification → issues → context-aware recommendations

It forms the foundation for:

- controlled self-adjustment loops (v94)
- policy-driven correction strategies
- benchmark-informed system adaptation

All changes are additive, deterministic, and fully inspectable.

title: v93.0.0 — Deterministic Self-Diagnostics & Recommendation Layer
body:
## [v93.0.0] — Deterministic Self-Diagnostics & Recommendation Layer

Introduces a deterministic self-diagnostics and recommendation layer
that analyzes benchmark and correction metrics to identify system issues
and propose structured improvements.

This release marks the transition from measurement and benchmarking to
explicit system introspection and reasoning.

### Added

- Self-diagnostics module (src/qec/analysis/self_diagnostics.py):
  - `normalize_results()`:
    supports both raw benchmark outputs and summarized results
  - `aggregate_metrics()`:
    deterministic aggregation by (dfa_type, n, mode)
  - `best_mode_per_system()`:
    stable mode selection using deterministic tie-breaking
  - `detect_issues()`:
    rule-based issue detection:
      - no_effect
      - over_correction
      - destabilizing
      - low_diversity
      - globally_weak_correction
  - `recommend()`:
    deterministic issue → recommendation mapping
  - `run_self_diagnostics()`:
    full analysis pipeline
  - `print_diagnostics()`:
    sorted, text-based output for inspection

### Testing

- Added comprehensive diagnostics test suite (42 tests):
  - input normalization (raw + summary)
  - aggregation correctness
  - issue detection rules
  - recommendation mapping
  - deterministic outputs across runs
  - no mutation of input data

### Key Outcomes

- Enables system-level introspection of correction behavior
- Provides explicit, reproducible reasoning about system performance
- Establishes deterministic issue taxonomy
- Introduces structured recommendation engine

### System Impact

This release upgrades the system from benchmarking to self-diagnosis:

trajectory → correction → benchmarking → diagnostics → recommendations

It forms the foundation for:

- benchmark-aware diagnostics (v93.1)
- controlled self-adjustment (v94)
- policy-driven correction strategies

No changes to correction or benchmark logic.
All additions are strictly additive and deterministic.

title: v92.3.1 — Benchmark API Stabilization & Explicit Aggregation
body:
## [v92.3.1] — Benchmark API Stabilization & Explicit Aggregation

Refines the v92.3 benchmark framework by removing implicit behavior,
standardizing DFA builder interfaces, and enforcing explicit,
deterministic aggregation.

### Changed

- Standardized all DFA builders to accept `(n=None)` signature
  - Parameterized DFAs assert `n is not None`
  - Fixed-topology DFAs accept and ignore `n`

- Replaced implicit builder introspection with explicit calls:
  - Removed `__code__.co_varnames` usage
  - All builders now invoked uniformly via `builder(n)`

- Introduced `DFA_SIZES` registry:
  - Explicit mapping of DFA types to valid sizes
  - Separates topology definition from experiment configuration

### Improved

- Summary aggregation now explicitly groups by `(dfa_type, n)`
- Deterministic ordering enforced using `str(n)` for stable sorting
- Print output clarified:
  - `DFA: chain (n=10)`
  - `DFA: two_basin (n=NA)`

### Testing

- Added tests for:
  - builder API consistency (`n` argument)
  - explicit summary key structure
  - handling of `n=None` cases
  - print output formatting for size-less DFAs

- Updated existing tests to match new summary structure

### System Impact

This patch removes implicit contracts and ensures that benchmark
structure is fully explicit and deterministic, providing a stable
foundation for the upcoming self-diagnostics and recommendation layer (v93).

No changes to correction logic or benchmark results.
All updates are structural and backward-compatible.

title: ## [v92.3.0] — Multi-DFA Benchmark Suite & Efficiency Metrics
body:
## [v92.3.0] — Multi-DFA Benchmark Suite & Efficiency Metrics

Introduces a deterministic multi-DFA benchmarking framework with normalized
efficiency metrics and alignment visualization, enabling cross-system
comparison of correction strategies.

This release marks the transition from single-system evaluation to
taxonomy-level analysis of correction behavior across DFA classes.

### Added

- Multi-DFA benchmark suite (src/qec/experiments/dfa_benchmark.py):
  - Deterministic DFA generators:
    - chain
    - cycle
    - branching
    - two_basin
    - dead_state
  - Benchmark modes:
    - none
    - square
    - d4
    - d4 + invariant-guided correction
  - Full suite runner across DFA types and sizes
  - Deterministic result aggregation and summary tables

- Alignment visualization (src/qec/visualization/alignment_plot.py):
  - Step-by-step state / syndrome before/after tables
  - Compact "changes-only" view for correction analysis
  - Fully deterministic text-based output

### Enhanced

- Correction metrics (src/qec/experiments/correction_layer.py):
  - Added `compression_efficiency`:
    normalized reduction in syndrome diversity
  - Added `stability_efficiency`:
    normalized stability gain relative to baseline
  - Introduced `safe_div()` for zero-safe metric computation

### Testing

- Added comprehensive benchmark test suite (39 tests):
  - Deterministic DFA generation
  - Stable ordering guarantees
  - Reproducibility across runs
  - Efficiency metric bounds [0,1]
  - No mutation of inputs
  - Alignment and visualization output validation

### Key Outcomes

- Enables direct comparison of correction modes across DFA classes
- Establishes foundation for correction taxonomy:
  e.g. chain vs cycle vs branching behavior differences
- Provides normalized metrics for cross-system evaluation
- Introduces alignment inspection for debugging correction dynamics

### System Impact

This release upgrades the system from single-instance analysis to
deterministic multi-system benchmarking, forming the foundation for:

- self-diagnostics (v93)
- recommendation systems
- benchmark-aware correction strategies

No changes to decoder core or existing pipeline behavior.
All additions are strictly additive and deterministic.

title: v87.4.0 — Attractor Basin Decomposition
body:
🚀 v87.4.0 — Attractor Basin Decomposition
Overview

This release introduces basin detection as a new deterministic analysis layer, extending the system from attractor identification to full phase-space partitioning.

Using strongly connected components (SCC) over the state graph, the system now identifies regions of attraction, computes basin-level metrics, and classifies dynamical structure at a higher level of abstraction.

✨ Added
Phase Basin Analysis

src/qec/experiments/phase_basin_analysis.py

Deterministic adjacency construction from state_graph
Strongly connected component (SCC) detection via Tarjan’s algorithm
Interpretation of SCCs as attractor basins
Basin Metrics

For each basin:

size — number of states in the basin
mass — sum of attractor field scores
coherence — ratio of internal vs outgoing transitions

These metrics quantify both structural scale and dynamical stability.

Basin Classification

Each basin is classified into:

fixed_point — single-state basin with self-loop
oscillatory — small cyclic basin (size = 2)
stable_basin — high internal coherence
transient_basin — low coherence / high leakage

Includes a deterministic confidence score per basin.

State Mapping

Deterministic mapping:

state → basin_id
Enables downstream analysis at the basin level.
🔗 Integration

Updated:

src/qec/experiments/phase_trajectory_analysis.py

Added new output key:

"basin_analysis": {
  "basins": [...],
  "mapping": {...},
  "n_basins": int
}
Consumes:
state_graph (v87.2)
attractor_field (v87.3)
No recomputation or mutation of prior layers
🧪 Tests

New test suite:

tests/test_phase_basin_analysis.py (14 tests)

Covers:

SCC correctness
Single-node and multi-node basins
Coherence calculation
Classification rules
Mapping completeness
Deterministic output
No-mutation guarantees
🧠 Conceptual Impact

This release upgrades the system from:

detecting attractors

to:

partitioning the dynamical field into regions of attraction

The system now models:

trajectory → graph → field → basins

Enabling:

basin-level stability analysis
structural interpretation of dynamics
foundation for clustering and symbolic grammar
⚙️ Guarantees
Fully deterministic (no randomness)
Pure post-processing layer
Additive integration (no breaking changes)
No mutation of existing pipeline outputs
🔜 Next Steps

v87.7 — Cross-Trajectory Analysis

Scales total score to [0, 1] using fixed maximum

Enables cross-run and cross-version comparison

v82.8.0 introduces a deterministic hybrid inverse design engine that maps target invariant behaviors to concrete (theta, sequence) configurations.

Added

Hybrid inverse design engine (src/qec/experiments/hybrid_inverse_design.py)

build_target_spec(target) — deterministic invariant-space target definitions (stable, fragile, chaotic, boundary_rider)

Structured output format

Includes top_k, best_pair, and full score_distribution

Per-entry fields: theta, sequence, score, compatibility, alignment, class, phase

Demo script

scripts/run_hybrid_inverse_design.py

Demonstrates evaluation across all four target behaviors using a small deterministic grid

Comprehensive test suite

32 tests covering determinism, ordering, invalid handling, edge cases, and scoring behavior

Fully deterministic (no randomness)

No mutation or adaptive search

Bounded candidate space

Strict reuse of existing hybrid co-design engine

Invariant-aligned scoring model

Outcome

This release transforms the system from a hybrid evaluation engine into a deterministic inverse design framework, enabling reproducible mapping from desired invariant behavior to candidate constructions.

title: 🔖 v82.7.1 — Hybrid Co-Design Hardening Pass
body:
🔖 v82.7.1 — Hybrid Co-Design Hardening Pass
Summary

Refines the Hybrid Co-Design Engine with explicit thresholds, validity-aware selection rules, invariant safety checks, and agreement metrics—improving interpretability and robustness without changing behavior.

Added

Alignment agreement metrics for hybrid pairs:

phase_agreement — whether θ and sequence share the same phase

class_agreement — whether θ and sequence share the same behavioral class

Additional tests in tests/test_hybrid_codesign.py:

test_best_pair_excludes_invalid

test_phase_class_agreement_fields

Changed

Alignment thresholds now explicit constants:

ALIGN_THRESHOLD = 2.0
NEAR_THRESHOLD = 5.0

Replaces inline “magic numbers” for clarity and future tuning.

Best pair selection is now validity-aware:

best_pair is selected from valid pairs only (alignment != "invalid")

Falls back to {} if no valid pairs exist

Worst pair selection includes all pairs:

Ensures failure modes (including invalid pairs) are always surfaced

Safety

Added invariant assertions in score_hybrid_pair():

distance >= 0.0

0.0 <= compatibility <= 1.0

These guard against upstream regressions in embedding or scoring logic.

Notes

No changes to core algorithms or scoring behavior

No new dependencies

Fully deterministic

Existing functionality preserved

Outcome

Hybrid co-design is now:

More interpretable (agreement metrics)

More robust (assertions + explicit thresholds)

More consistent (clear best/worst semantics)

title: 🚀 v82.6.2 — Post-Normalization Hardening
body:
🚀 v82.6.2 — Post-Normalization Hardening

Title: Safe Failure Boundaries

🔍 Overview

v82.6.2 introduces a minimal hardening fix to ensure robust handling of optional native dependencies following the import normalization in v82.6.1.

🧩 Fix
🔧 Execution Proof Stability

Updated exception handling in:

src/qec/controller/execution_proof.py

Change:

except Exception

→

except BaseException
🧠 Rationale

Certain native dependency failures (e.g. cryptography via pyo3) raise exceptions that do not inherit from Exception.

This previously caused:

import-time crashes

script failures unrelated to core logic

The fix ensures:

graceful degradation

proper error messaging

no impact on deterministic behavior

✅ Results

All core imports succeed:

qec

qec.experiments

qec.diagnostics

cross_domain_mapper

midi_cube_bridge

Scripts:

import cleanly

fail only when signing is invoked

emit clear RuntimeError messages

No changes required for:

mido (already lazy-loaded)

matplotlib (already optional)

6 audit tests passing

🔬 Impact

This release ensures:

optional dependencies cannot break import chains

native library failures are contained

system remains usable even in partially configured environments

🧠 Philosophy

“Optional dependencies should fail at use — not at import.”

curvature

QEC pipeline execution

🔍 Overview

v82.0.0 introduces the UFF ↔ QEC Bridge, the first integration layer connecting:

UFF (Unified Field Framework) — dynamics generation

QEC (Quantum Error Correction toolkit) — invariant analysis, control, and verification

This release establishes a closed experimental loop:

physics → features → invariants → verification → proof → consensus

The bridge is intentionally minimal and deterministic, acting purely as an adapter layer — not a replacement for the underlying physics models.

🧩 Core Additions
New Module

Velocity generation

Deterministic by construction — no randomness

Adapter-only architecture — no physics reimplementation

🔒 Boundary Integrity

title: v81.1.0 — Signed Execution Proofs (Deterministic Attestation Layer)
body:
📝 Release Notes
v81.1.0 — Signed Execution Proofs (Deterministic Attestation Layer)

This release introduces a cryptographic proof layer for QEC execution, enabling deterministic runs to be signed, verified, and attributed without introducing randomness or external dependencies.

Added

Execution proof module

src/qec/controller/execution_proof.py

Provides end-to-end proof construction:

build_proof_payload() — canonical, deterministic payload construction

serialize_proof_payload() — stable JSON → bytes encoding

sign_payload() — Ed25519 signing (deterministic)

verify_signature() — signature validation (True / False)

create_execution_proof() — full pipeline: build → sign → verify → optional write

Canonical proof payload

{
  "signer_id": "...",
  "final_hash": "...",
  "steps": ...,
  "match": true,
  "mismatch_index": null,
  "metadata": {...}
}

Design guarantees:

sorted keys

no hidden entropy

no automatic timestamps or UUIDs

identical inputs → identical payload bytes

Deterministic signing (Ed25519)

Same key + same payload → identical signature

No randomness required

No external infrastructure

Verification layer

Payload integrity verified via signature

Tampering of payload or signature is detected reliably

Verification returns boolean (no exceptions for invalid signatures)

Runner script

FSM → replay → proof → signature → verification

Outputs:

MATCH: True
FINAL HASH: <hash>

VERIFIED: True

signature validity

full proof lifecycle

no mutation guarantees

Guarantees

signed computational artifacts

title: v80.1.0 — Adaptive Threshold FSM (Deterministic Self-Tuning)
body:
📝 Release Notes

v80.1.0 — Adaptive Threshold FSM (Deterministic Self-Tuning)

This release introduces deterministic adaptive thresholds to the QEC FSM Controller, enabling the system to self-calibrate based on recent execution history while preserving full reproducibility.

Added

Adaptive threshold mechanism

Introduced:

_adapt_thresholds(history, stability_threshold, boundary_threshold, window=5)

A pure, deterministic function that updates thresholds based on recent behavior:

Stability threshold
Exponential moving average:

0.8 * old + 0.2 * mean(recent stability_scores)

Boundary crossing threshold
Adjusted using reject pressure:

Increase by +0.1 if reject rate > 0.5

Decrease by -0.1 if reject rate < 0.2

Both thresholds are clamped to [0, 10]

Changed

FSM now self-tunes during execution

Threshold adaptation occurs only during continuation loops
(EVALUATE → ANALYZE)

No adaptation during:

INIT

ACCEPT

REJECT

This preserves decision integrity while allowing gradual calibration.

History trace enriched with threshold state

Continuation steps now include:

"thresholds": {
  "stability": float,
  "boundary": float
}

This enables full replay and audit of threshold evolution.

Guarantees

Fully deterministic (no randomness introduced)

No changes to FSM state structure

No mutation of input data

Convergence detection (v80.0.2) remains intact

Behavior identical for short histories (< window size)

Tests

8 new tests added (total: 54 passing)

Coverage includes:

deterministic threshold updates

bounds enforcement

no input mutation

correct adaptation timing (continue loops only)

convergence compatibility

trend alignment with mean stability

reject pressure influence

Why this matters

This release upgrades the FSM from a static controller to a:

deterministic adaptive control system

It enables:

self-calibration without stochastic methods

stable long-run behavior under varying inputs

fully traceable threshold evolution

Forward Path

This lays the foundation for:

v80.5 — Neural ODE trajectory modeling

v81 — smart-contract-style deterministic replay

v82+ — automated invariant discovery

body:
🚀 v74.6.0 — Invariant Sonification Engine

Structure → Signal (Math → Sound Layer)

Introduces a deterministic sonification layer that converts:

invariant structure

stability metrics

phase classification

into audible signals

This release completes the pipeline:

perturbation → invariants → sound

✨ Added
🎧 Invariant Sonification Engine

New module:

src/qec/experiments/invariant_sonification.py

Implements a fully deterministic mapping from mathematical structure to audio signal.

🎵 Frequency Mapping (Feature Identity)

Each feature is mapped to a fixed base frequency:

energy → 220 Hz

centroid → 330 Hz

spread → 440 Hz

zcr → 550 Hz

No scaling or normalization — identity is preserved.

🔊 Amplitude (Stability Encoding)
amplitude = 1 / (1 + stability_score)

stable systems → louder

unstable systems → quieter

Encodes a Lyapunov-like stability measure into signal intensity.

🌊 Modulation (Sensitivity Encoding)

mean drift → tremolo rate (0–20 Hz)

low drift → smooth tone

high drift → rapid modulation

Represents perturbation sensitivity as temporal variation.

🧠 Harmonic Structure (Invariant Strength)

strong invariant → pure sine

weak invariant → sine + small harmonic

non-invariant → multi-harmonic

Encodes invariant classification directly into waveform structure.

⚡ Phase Classification → Envelope

Phase regions map to temporal envelopes:

stable_region → sustained tone

near_boundary → pulsing

unstable_region → decaying

chaotic_transition → irregular bursts

Provides an audible representation of phase-space behavior.

🎚 Signal Generation
generate_invariant_signal(analysis)

combines feature tones

applies modulation + envelope

normalizes output to [-1, 1]

deterministic phase alignment

concatenates multiple analyses

represents system evolution over time

implemented using stdlib wave

no external dependencies

title: 🚀 v74.5.0 — Invariant Engine  Stability, Phase Structure & Lyapunov Proxy Layer
body:
🚀 v74.5.0 — Invariant Engine

Stability, Phase Structure & Lyapunov Proxy Layer

Introduces the Invariant Engine, a deterministic analysis layer that converts perturbation data into:

✨ Added
🧠 Invariant Analysis Engine

src/qec/experiments/invariant_engine.py

Provides a structured interpretation of perturbation probe outputs.

📉 Stability Score (Lyapunov Proxy)
compute_stability_score(probe)

Defines a scalar stability metric:

(1 - stable_ratio) + 0.5 × boundary_crossings + Σ(mean_drift)

lower → more stable

higher → more unstable

Acts as a Lyapunov-like proxy, estimating how the system responds to small perturbations.

🔒 Invariant Identification
identify_invariants(probe, threshold)

Classifies features into:

strong_invariants → minimal drift

weak_invariants → low drift

non_invariants → unstable features

All features are always categorized, ensuring full coverage.

🧭 Phase Classification
classify_phase(probe)

Maps perturbation behavior into phase-space regions:

stable_region

near_boundary

unstable_region

chaotic_transition

Derived from:

stability ratio

boundary crossings

aggregate drift

📊 Feature Sensitivity Ranking
rank_features(probe)

Returns features sorted by robustness:

most stable → least stable

Provides a direct ordering of candidate invariants.

title: 🚀 v74.4.0 — Perturbation Probe Layer
body:
🚀 v74.4.0 — Perturbation Probe Layer

Invariant Sensitivity Engine

Introduces a deterministic perturbation-based analysis layer that measures how system outputs respond to small input changes.

This release marks the transition from:

static classification
to
dynamic stability analysis

✨ Added
🧪 Perturbation Probe Engine

New module:

src/qec/experiments/perturbation_probe.py

Implements a full perturbation workflow:

deterministic input perturbation

pipeline re-execution

stability and sensitivity measurement

🔬 Deterministic Perturbation Generation

Applies small symmetric perturbations:

delta = epsilon * ((i % 3) - 1)

Targets core features:

energy

spectral centroid

spectral spread

zero-crossing rate

Ensures:

reproducibility

zero-centered variation

no stochastic noise

🔁 Pipeline Re-evaluation

Each perturbed sample is passed through:

compare_sonic_features()

classify_comparison()

No modification to existing logic.

📊 Stability Metrics

Introduces quantitative sensitivity measures:

stable_ratio
→ fraction of perturbations preserving classification

boundary_crossings
→ number of classification flips (instability detection)

mean_drift
→ average feature deviation under perturbation

most_sensitive / most_stable
→ feature-level sensitivity ranking

🧠 Mathematical Interpretation

This module operationalizes perturbation theory:

Small parameter ε → controlled system variation

Smooth response → regular perturbation regime

abrupt classification flips → singular perturbation / boundary behavior

Perturbation theory studies how small changes influence solutions, often revealing stability structure and hidden regimes .

The probe effectively detects:

local stability regions

instability boundaries

sensitivity gradients

📦 Output
{
  "stable_ratio": float,
  "boundary_crossings": int,
  "mean_drift": {...},
  "most_sensitive": str,
  "most_stable": str
}

perturbation_summary.json
🧪 Testing

New test suite:

tests/test_perturbation_probe.py

~28 tests (all passing)

determinism verified

no-mutation guarantees

edge-case coverage (zero epsilon, large epsilon, flips)

integration with classification pipeline

Total suite: 4236+ tests passing

🧠 Design Philosophy

This is not noise injection.

This is a controlled stability probe:

deterministic

symmetric

minimal

interpretable

The system now measures:

not just what state it is in
but how fragile that state is

🔥 Key Outcome

Enables detection of:

stable regimes (high stability ratio)

boundary layers (classification flips)

instability drivers (high-drift features)

This aligns with classical stability analysis, where a system is considered stable if small perturbations do not amplify over time .

🔜 Next

title: 🚀 v74.3.0 — Dual-Lattice Invariant Visualizer
body:
🚀 v74.3.0 — Dual-Lattice Invariant Visualizer

Introduces a dual-structure visualization system that cleanly separates signal measurement space from state interpretation space.

This release transforms sonic analysis outputs into structured, spatial representations without introducing ambiguity or visual noise.

✨ Added
Dual-Lattice Visualization System

Two complementary lattice structures with strictly defined roles:

🧊 Rubik Lattice (8×8×8) — Signal / Measurement Field

Encodes spectral structure as a discrete 3D voxel field.

x-axis → frequency bins (FFT peaks)

y-axis → normalized energy levels

z-axis → channel / slice index

color mapping → deterministic 8-band spectral mapping (low → high frequency)

activation threshold → filters inactive voxels

Represents the measured structure of the signal, not interpretation.

🔺 Sierpinski Lattice (3×3×3) — Interpretation / State Field

Encodes system state using a fixed tetrahedral topology (14 nodes).

Each node is assigned a single classification:

🔵 stable

🟡 divergent

⚪ transition

🔴 collapse

🟢 recovery

Assignments are derived proportionally from sequence_analysis.json.

No color blending or ambiguity — each node maps to exactly one state.

🧠 Strict Separation of Roles

Rubik lattice → what the system measures

Sierpinski lattice → what the system concludes

Prevents:

visual overloading

mixed semantics

“rainbow cube explosion” failure mode

📦 Outputs

Generated in:

artifacts/sonic_visualization/

rubik_frame_*.png — voxelized spectral field

sierpinski_state.png — state topology

combined_view.png — aligned dual-lattice view

🧪 Testing

27 tests, all passing

Deterministic outputs verified

No mutation of input data

Structural correctness validated for both lattices

🧠 Design Philosophy

This is not a visualization gimmick.

This is a structural representation layer:

Rubik lattice approximates a discretized signal/phase field

Sierpinski lattice encodes state topology / regime classification

Together they form a bridge between:

spectral analysis

state transitions

spatial reasoning

🔜 Next

v74.4 introduces perturbation probing (Applied Mathematics — Chapter 2):

measure sensitivity to small changes

detect boundary layers

begin automatic invariant discovery

title: v74.2.0 — Directional Sonic State Classification
body:
## [v74.2.0] — 2026-03-19
Directional Sonic State Classification

Upgrades the sonic comparison system from magnitude-based change detection
to directional classification of state transitions.

This enables identification of structural behaviors such as divergence,
collapse, and recovery in audio-derived systems.

---

### Improved

**Sonic Comparison Module**

- Added derived metrics:
  - energy_ratio
  - spread_ratio
  - centroid_delta_signed
  - fft_peak_count_change

- Replaced magnitude-only classification with directional rules:

| Classification | Description |
|----------------|------------|
| stable | minimal change across all metrics |
| divergent | expansion of spectral spread with stable energy |
| transition | large structural reorganization (low FFT similarity) |
| collapse | energy loss with increasing spectral diffusion |
| recovery | re-stabilization with reduced spread and preserved structure |

---

### Results

Applied to the QSOL 5-state trajectory:

- 1→2: divergent
- 2→3: transition
- 3→4: collapse
- 4→5: recovery

Classification aligns with labeled system states without hardcoding.

---

### Tests

- 29 tests (up from 25)
- Added:
  - directional classification coverage
  - derived metric validation
  - determinism verification

All tests pass.

---

### Guarantees

- Fully deterministic
- No mutation of inputs
- No changes to underlying feature extraction
- Minimal, scoped refinement only

---

### Architecture Progression

- v74.0.0 — sonic artifact analysis
- v74.1.0 — comparison + trajectory detection
- v74.2.0 — directional state classification

---

### Result

The system now identifies the *type* of structural change in audio
signals, enabling trajectory-level interpretation of system dynamics.

---

### Next

v75.0.0 — quantum invariant search harness

Regression Detection & Cross-Version Comparison

title: v72.3.0 — Deterministic Sonification Comparison Framework
body:
## [v72.3.0] — 2026-03-19
Deterministic Sonification Comparison Framework (Baseline vs Multidimensional)

Introduces a fully deterministic experimental framework for comparing
E8 sonification methods under identical inputs.

This release establishes the measurement layer of the sonification
pipeline, enabling quantitative evaluation of invariant preservation,
signal structure, and multidimensional expressiveness.

All comparisons are reproducible, non-mutating, and rely only on numpy
and Python standard library.

---

### Added

**Sonification Comparison Framework** (`src/qec/experiments/`)

- `sonification_comparison.py`
  - `run_sonification_comparison(result, output_dir=None)`
  - Runs:
    - `sonify_e8_baseline` (v72.1.0)
    - `sonify_e8_multidim` (v72.2.0)
  - Ensures identical inputs for both methods

---

### Metrics

The following deterministic metrics are computed:

**1. Invariant Silence Fidelity**
- Fraction of invariant-region samples that are exactly zero
- Reported for baseline and multidimensional outputs
- Expected: `1.0` (strict invariant preservation)

**2. Cross-Channel Leakage (Multidimensional)**
- Detects non-zero samples inside invariant mask
- Expected: `0.0`

**3. Structural Energy (RMS)**
- RMS energy for:
  - baseline
  - multidimensional channel 0
  - multidimensional channel 1

**4. Channel Separation**
- Pearson correlation between multidimensional channels
- Bounded in [-1, 1]

**5. Spectral Spread (Variance Proxy)**
- Variance of signals as a deterministic proxy for signal complexity

---

### Artifact Output (Optional)

When `output_dir` is provided:

- `baseline.wav`
- `multidim.wav`
- `comparison.json`

All artifacts are reproducible and deterministic.

---

### Tests

- 24 tests across:
  - determinism guarantees
  - non-mutation of input
  - invariant preservation (`silence_fidelity == 1.0`)
  - zero leakage
  - bounded correlation
  - finite numeric outputs
  - artifact generation
  - edge cases (empty invariants, single column, zero complexity)

All tests pass.

---

### Guarantees

- Fully deterministic (no randomness)
- No mutation of input data
- No dependency on external audio or FFT libraries
- No modification of sonification implementations

---

### Architecture

This release introduces the **measurement layer** of the sonification system:

- v72.1.0 — baseline sonification (mono)
- v72.2.0 — multidimensional sonification (stereo)
- v72.3.0 — comparative measurement framework

This enables reproducible evaluation of:

- invariant preservation
- structural integrity
- multidimensional signal behavior

---

### Notes

- Metrics are amplitude-based and do not capture phase relationships
- Invariant validation is assumed correct upstream; this module measures compliance only

These considerations are deferred to future versions.

---

### Next

v72.4.0 — Interpretation Layer

- metric aggregation and scoring
- comparison classification (baseline vs multidimensional)
- experiment batching and summary statistics

title: 🚀 v72.2.0 — Multidimensional Invariant-Preserving Sonification
body:
🚀 v72.2.0 — Multidimensional Invariant-Preserving Sonification
Summary

Extends the deterministic E8 baseline sonification into a multidimensional audio representation, introducing qudit-inspired encodings (qutrit, ququart) while preserving all core system invariants.

This release establishes a structured, layered sonification operator that separates baseline structural information from higher-dimensional embeddings in a reproducible and invariant-safe manner.

✨ Added
🎧 Multidimensional sonification module

src/qec/modules/sonification/e8_multidim.py

sonify_e8_multidim(result, output_path=None) -> np.ndarray

Returns deterministic stereo output:

Channel 0 — Baseline (Structural Layer)

E8 root ratio sine mapping (re-implemented inline)

Error-rate-driven bounded detune

Complexity-weighted amplitude

Channel 1 — Multidimensional Layer

Qutrit encoding (base-3)
Pulsed sine mapping via BASE_FREQ * (CUSTOM_PI ** value)

Ququart encoding (base-4)
Triangle-wave harmonic mapping via (BASE_FREQ / PHI) * (value + 1)

Temporal lattice via evenly partitioned pulse structure

⚖️ Energy Distribution

Channel 0 amplitude: (1 - complexity)

Channel 1 amplitude: complexity

Provides a semantic split between structural stability and higher-dimensional complexity.

🔇 Invariant-Preserving Silence Gates

Applied after full stereo composition

Enforced on both channels simultaneously

Inclusive boundaries: (t >= start) & (t <= end)

Ensures global structural silence, not per-layer masking artifacts.

📊 Signal Processing Guarantees

Single-pass normalization after full composition

Output bounded to int16 range

No NaN / inf values

Fully deterministic output

🧪 Test suite (33 tests)

Covers:

Determinism

Input immutability

Stereo output shape (n_samples, 2)

Silence zone enforcement across channels

Numeric safety (no NaN / inf)

Output bounds (int16)

Channel energy split behavior

Invalid input rejection

WAV export correctness

Edge-case handling

🧠 Design Principles

Deterministic by construction
Identical input → identical stereo output

Non-mutating execution
Input structures remain unchanged

Invariant-preserving composition
Silence applied at the global signal level

Layered interpretability
Channel separation maintains structural vs multidimensional meaning

Numerically stable mappings
Log-domain modulation and bounded frequency scaling

Minimal dependencies
NumPy + Python standard library only

🔬 Technical Notes

Qutrit and ququart mappings use distinct frequency bases (CUSTOM_PI, PHI) to ensure channel independence

Temporal pulse structure is derived from sequence length, eliminating drift

All generated frequencies remain safely below Nyquist limits under bounded detune scaling

Baseline logic is re-implemented inline to maintain control over normalization and composition order

⚠️ Breaking Changes

None.
This is a strictly additive module with no impact on existing pipeline or baseline sonification.

🧩 Position in System

This release extends:

v72.1.0 — Deterministic baseline sonification operator

into:

v72.2.0 — Multidimensional, invariant-preserving audio representation

Together they form:

A reference baseline (v72.1)

A higher-dimensional embedding layer (v72.2)

🔜 Next

Potential directions:

Comparative analysis: baseline vs multidimensional perceptual clarity

Invariant detection via audio features

Higher-dimensional extensions (qudit generalization, spatial encoding)

Dataset generation for reproducible audio experiments

title: 🚀 v72.1.0 — Deterministic E8 Sonification Baseline
body:
🚀 v72.1.0 — Deterministic E8 Sonification Baseline
Summary

Introduces a minimal, deterministic sonification engine that maps decoder experiment outputs into audio using a structurally faithful E8-inspired harmonic embedding.

This release establishes a stable, invariant-preserving baseline operator for sonification, designed for reproducibility, interpretability, and zero external dependencies.

✨ Added
🎧 Sonification module

src/qec/modules/sonification/e8_baseline.py

sonify_e8_baseline(result, output_path=None) -> np.ndarray

Implements a deterministic mapping:

Columns → Frequency
E8 root ratios mapped via BASE_FREQ * ratio

Error Rate → Pitch Modulation
log(error_rate + 1) * 1200, applied as bounded per-voice detune

Complexity → Amplitude
(1 - complexity) / N, clamped to [0, 1]

Invariants → Silence Gates
Hard zero mask applied over [start, end] intervals (inclusive)

Normalization
Single-pass normalization after full signal assembly

WAV Output (optional)
Standard library only (wave, struct)

🧪 Test suite (21 tests)

Deterministic output verification

Input immutability guarantees

Silence zone correctness (inclusive boundaries)

Numeric safety (no NaN/inf)

Output bounded to int16 range

Invalid input rejection (strict contract)

🧠 Design Principles

Deterministic by construction
Same input → identical audio output

Non-mutating execution
Input data structures remain unchanged

Invariant-preserving
Silence gates applied post-aggregation to enforce structural cuts

Numerically stable
Log-domain modulation avoids singularities and runaway scaling

Minimal dependencies
NumPy + Python standard library only

🔬 Technical Notes

Detune scaling is explicitly bounded (~1.55× worst-case frequency shift)

Invariant masking uses consistent inclusive boundaries in both implementation and tests

All floating-point operations are safe for error_rate ≥ 0

⚠️ Breaking Changes

None.
This is a new module with no impact on existing pipeline behavior.

🧩 Position in System

This release establishes:

A canonical baseline sonification operator for QEC pipelines

It serves as:

A reference for future sonification extensions (v72.2+)

A reproducible artifact generator for experiments

A debugging and analysis tool for invariant behavior

🔜 Next

v72.2 — Multidimensional Sonification Engine

Planned extensions:

Qutrit / qudit-inspired mappings

Multi-channel / spatial encoding

Higher-dimensional lattice embeddings

Poly-rhythmic and temporal topology coupling

title: v71.0.3 — Immutability Guard (Dev-Only) + Pipeline Invariant Enforcement
body:
v71.0.3 introduces a dev-only immutability guard for the pipeline, enabling
runtime enforcement of the system’s non-mutating execution invariant.

This is a zero-behavior-change hardening release focused on strengthening
correctness guarantees without impacting performance.

--------------------------------------------------
Added
--------------------------------------------------

- Dev-only immutability guard in `build_full_pipeline(...)`
  - Controlled via environment variable: QEC_DEBUG_IMMUTABILITY=1
  - Captures pre-execution snapshot of `suites` using deepcopy
  - Asserts post-execution equality to detect mutation leaks
  - Completely inert when disabled (default)

--------------------------------------------------
Behavior
--------------------------------------------------

- No changes to pipeline outputs
- No changes to execution flow
- No performance impact in normal mode
- Determinism preserved

--------------------------------------------------
Testing
--------------------------------------------------

- All 3950 tests pass in normal mode
- All 3950 tests pass with QEC_DEBUG_IMMUTABILITY=1 enabled
- Existing immutability tests remain in place
- Guard provides additional runtime verification layer

--------------------------------------------------
Context
--------------------------------------------------

This builds on v71.0.2 (non-mutating pipeline + centralized validation),
elevating the non-mutation guarantee from a design assumption to an
optionally enforced runtime invariant.

The pipeline is now:
- Structurally validated
- Non-mutating by design
- Test-verified
- Runtime-enforceable (when enabled)

--------------------------------------------------
Notes
--------------------------------------------------

- Deepcopy is intentionally scoped to debug mode only to avoid unnecessary cost
- Stage modules remain read-only on input structures (documented invariant)
- Future mutation regressions will be caught immediately when guard is enabled

--------------------------------------------------

title: v71.0.1 — Pipeline Hardening & Contract Enforcement
body:
🧠 Summary

v71.0.1 introduces a hardening pass across the modular pipeline, converting implicit assumptions into explicit, enforced invariants.

This release does not change behavior or outputs, but significantly improves:

input validation

error transparency

pipeline correctness

mutation safety

It represents the transition from a working modular system → a contract-driven execution pipeline.

✨ Added
🔒 Explicit Pipeline Mode Validation

Enforces:

mode ∈ {"single", "sweep"}

Invalid modes now raise:

ValueError("Invalid mode ...")
📏 Suite Cardinality Invariants

single mode → exactly 1 suite required

sweep mode → at least 1 suite required

Prevents silent misinterpretation of inputs.

🛡️ Sweep Result Validation

Validates presence of:

result["results"]

Ensures:

correct type (list)

Raises clear ValueError instead of:

KeyError

TypeError

🧼 Improved
📢 Error Diagnostics (Pareto Module)

Replaced generic errors:

"unknown scenario"

"no valid numeric deltas"

With contextual messages:

include offending scenario

list available scenarios

clarify failure conditions

🧹 Module Boundary Cleanup

Removed unused imports from benchmark_stress.py

Confirms pipeline as the single orchestration entry point

🧬 Fixed
⚠️ Mutation Bug in Pipeline

single mode previously mutated input suite dict

Now uses:

result = suites[0].copy()

✅ Ensures:

referential safety

no side effects

safe reuse of inputs

🔁 Behavior & Compatibility

✅ No change to outputs

✅ Determinism preserved

✅ All 110 tests pass

✅ Backward-compatible API

🧠 Architectural Impact

v71.0.1 formalizes the pipeline as a validated transformation system:

Inputs are now strictly checked

Invalid states are rejected early

Execution is side-effect safe

This lays the foundation for:

invariant-driven execution (v71+)

composable pipelines

title: v68.8.0 — Dark-State Invariants  Invariant-Driven Computation Analysis Layer
body:
Summary

Introduces Dark-State Invariants, a deterministic framework for identifying inactive regions in BP message dynamics.

This release shifts focus from:

what changes

to:

what no longer needs to change

Dark-state detection enables direct measurement of computational redundancy, laying the groundwork for future execution elimination and selective update strategies.

Added
🧠 Dark-State Detection

compute_dark_state_mask(llr_trace)

Returns per-timestep boolean masks indicating dark-stable nodes

A node is considered dark when:

sign(v_t) == sign(v_{t-1})

|v_t - v_{t-1}| < ε

Deterministic, vectorized, float64-safe implementation

No randomness, no decoder modification

📊 Dark-State Metrics

Added to every benchmark run:

mean_dark_fraction

Average fraction of dark nodes across all iterations

final_dark_fraction

Fraction of dark nodes at final iteration

These metrics quantify temporal invariance density in BP traces.

🧪 Test Coverage

Added TestDarkState suite (5 tests):

Deterministic mask generation

Correct sign-stability behavior

Magnitude threshold enforcement

Edge-case handling (short traces)

Integration with benchmark pipeline

All tests pass (33 total).

Design Principles

Layer 5 only — no decoder or diagnostic changes

Fully deterministic (seeded, reproducible)

Minimal implementation footprint

Zero new dependencies

Strict separation of measurement vs execution

Interpretation

Dark-state invariants measure:

where computation has effectively stopped contributing new information

Key insight:

Converging systems → high dark fraction

Oscillatory systems → low dark fraction

Noisy/chaotic systems → unstable dark fraction

This provides a third axis alongside:

cosine fidelity (directional stability)

sign fidelity (discrete stability)

Why This Matters

This release introduces a new optimization paradigm:

Invariant-Driven Computation Elimination

Rather than accelerating updates, the system can begin to:

identify stable regions

avoid redundant computation

focus effort on active information flow

Next Direction

This release enables:

Dark-state-aware update skipping (v68.9+)

Active-node frontier tracking

Deterministic compute reduction strategies

Integration with ternary/qutrit decoding logic

Version

v68.8.0

title: 🚀 v68.7.2 — Deterministic Benchmark & Fidelity Baseline
body:
🚀 v68.7.2 — Deterministic Benchmark & Fidelity Baseline
Overview

This release introduces a fully deterministic benchmark and stress-testing framework for Belief Propagation (BP) dynamics, enabling systematic evaluation of convergence behavior, instability regimes, and information-flow characteristics across controlled scenarios.

v68.7.2 establishes a reproducible experimental layer for QEC development, providing consistent metrics, scenario-driven diagnostics, and fidelity analysis without modifying decoder logic.

✨ Added
Deterministic Benchmark Framework

New module: src/qec/experiments/benchmark_stress.py

CLI entry point: scripts/run_benchmark_stress.py

Full test suite: tests/test_benchmark_stress.py (28 tests)

Features:

Fully deterministic execution (seeded via SHA-256 → 64-bit → PCG64)

Byte-stable JSON outputs (sort_keys=True)

Float64 consistency throughout all computations

Stress Scenario Suite (9 total)
Scenario	Description
converging_baseline	Smooth convergence (control case)
high_noise	Large perturbations each iteration
oscillating_period3	Period-3 oscillatory behavior
oscillating_period2	True sign-flipping oscillation
long_iteration	Slow convergence over extended iterations
small_window	Minimal trace (edge-case behavior)
large_window	High-dimensional, long-run dynamics
pathological_extreme	Extreme values, near-zero, mixed regimes
diverging	Exponential divergence
Fidelity Metrics (LLR Trace Consistency)

New metrics computed per run:

mean_cosine_fidelity
Directional consistency between consecutive LLR vectors

mean_sign_fidelity
Fraction of sign agreement between iterations

mean_quantum_fidelity
Hilbert-space proxy: squared overlap of normalized vectors

LLR statistics

max_abs_llr

mean_abs_llr

fidelity_interpretation

high, medium, low classification

These metrics quantify stability vs oscillation vs divergence at the information-flow level, independent of convergence labels.

Outcome Classification

Mapping from BP regimes → standardized outcomes:

Outcome	Source Regimes
converged	stable_convergence
oscillatory	oscillatory_convergence
unstable	metastable_state, correction_cycling
diverged	chaotic_behavior

Special handling:

trapping_set_regime classified using energy descent fraction

Unknown regimes default to unstable (robust fallback)

🛠 Fixed
Oscillation Correctness

Fixed period-2 oscillation bug

Now uses true sign flip:

sign = 1.0 if (t % 2 == 0) else -1.0

Verified via low sign-agreement tests

Pathological Trace Safety

Eliminated negative-size RNG calls for small n_vars

Safe bounds:

size = max(0, n_vars - 2 * quarter)
size = min(quarter, size)
if size > 0: ...
JSON Serialization Robustness

Added support for np.bool_ → native bool

Ensures all diagnostics outputs serialize safely

Updated docstring to match actual float behavior (no forced rounding)

Numeric Stability

Cosine fidelity uses per-norm clamping
(avoids bias from global epsilon on denominator)

🧪 Tests

28 total tests across:

determinism guarantees

trace generator correctness

classification stability

fidelity metric validity

edge cases (zero-norm, small traces, pathological inputs)

All tests pass.

⚙️ Design Principles

Deterministic by construction

No decoder modifications

Experiment-layer only (Layer 5)

Minimal dependencies

Float64 strictness for reproducibility

📊 Why this matters

This release introduces a measurement system for BP behavior, enabling:

reproducible stress testing

quantitative comparison across scenarios

identification of instability regimes

analysis of information-flow dynamics

It forms the foundation for future optimization work, including:

invariant-based execution

dark-state detection (latent stability)

adaptive update scheduling

title: v68.6.2 — INV-003 Hardening: Robust Cache-Key Correctness & Full Coverage
body:
[v68.6.2] — 2026-03-19

Cross-Call Cache Key Hardening + Invariant Completion (QSOL-BP-INV-003)

This release completes and hardens the implementation of QSOL-BP-INV-003, ensuring that cross-call deterministic reuse is not only correct but fully robust under real-world inputs.

🔬 Invariant

QSOL-BP-INV-003 — Cross-Call Deterministic Reuse

Let F = compute_bp_dynamics_metrics and cache_key(x) be the byte-level encoding produced by _make_cache_key.

If cache_key(x) == cache_key(y), then F(x) = F(y) under the current implementation.

This release strengthens the correctness and completeness of cache_key, ensuring the invariant holds across all supported input types.

⚙️ Key Improvements
1. Deterministic Energy Encoding

Replaced struct.pack with:

np.asarray(energy_trace, dtype=np.float64).ravel().tobytes()

Handles lists, arrays, and nested inputs uniformly

None / empty → explicitly encoded as b""

Ensures byte-level consistency across all input forms

2. Parameter Normalization

Added _normalize_param_value() to coerce NumPy scalars → Python primitives

Prevents cache-key divergence from type mismatches:

np.float64(5.0) vs 5.0

Ensures stable and canonical parameter encoding

3. Correction Vector Canonicalization

correction_vectors=None and [] are now semantically equivalent

Both map to identical cache-key representation (b"")

Non-empty vectors encoded deterministically via float64 byte representation

4. Cache-Key Integrity Testing

Strengthened tests to validate key-level correctness, not output coincidence

test_different_params_different_cache_entries now asserts:

key1 != key2

Aligns test logic directly with INV-003

5. Extended Test Coverage

Added new validation suites:

TestCorrectionVectorsCacheKey

verifies correction vectors affect cache key

verifies None ≡ [] equivalence

TestParamNormalization

ensures NumPy scalar normalization produces identical keys

Total test suite:

114 tests passing (0 failures)

All prior tests unchanged

🛡️ Safety Guarantees

No API changes

No behavioral changes

Bitwise-identical outputs preserved

Cache correctness independent of input type representation

Full mutation safety maintained (deepcopy isolation)

📊 Impact

Eliminates redundant computation across identical calls

Maintains deterministic reproducibility guarantees

Prevents subtle cache fragmentation bugs under mixed input types

⚠️ Scope

Applies under current implementation of compute_bp_dynamics_metrics

Cache remains per-process (non-persistent)

Byte-level equivalence assumes IEEE 754 float64 consistency

🧠 Summary

v68.6.2 completes the transition of QEC into an invariant-driven execution system, where:

correctness is defined formally

optimizations are proven, not assumed

reuse is guaranteed by construction

This release finalizes the invariant stack:

v68.4.1 — Algebraic invariants (execution identity)

v68.5.0 — Data invariants (trace-level reuse)

v68.6.2 — Execution-context invariants (cross-call reuse)

title: v68.5.0 — Trace-Indexed Sign/CRC Reuse (QSOL-BP-INV-002)
body:
📄 Release Notes
[v68.5.0] — 2026-03-19

Invariant-Driven Redundancy Elimination in BP Dynamics (QSOL-BP-INV-002)

This release introduces a formally validated invariant that eliminates redundant sign-vector and CRC32 computations across belief propagation (BP) dynamics diagnostics. The invariant exploits deterministic equivalence of per-trace-element transformations, enabling computation reuse without altering outputs, convergence behavior, or test coverage.

🔬 Invariant

QSOL-BP-INV-002 — Trace-Indexed Sign/CRC Reuse

Let T = [v₀, v₁, …, vₙ₋₁] be a normalized LLR trace. The transformations:

sign(vᵢ) = np.where(vᵢ < 0, -1, 1)

crc(vᵢ) = crc32(sign(vᵢ).tobytes())

are pure, deterministic, and depend only on the values of vᵢ.

Therefore, these values can be computed once per trace element and reused across all BP dynamics metrics without changing any outputs.

✅ Formal Validation

The invariant is validated at multiple levels:

Slice equivalence
Per-metric inputs match cached values (np.testing.assert_array_equal)

Byte equivalence
.tobytes() identity and CRC32 equality across cached and inline paths

Purity
_sign() and CRC32 verified deterministic and side-effect-free

Mutation safety
Cached arrays are immutable (writeable=False) and never modified

Window safety
Independent slicing verified across asymmetric window configurations

Behavioral equivalence
Cached vs uncached paths are bitwise identical

All validation tests pass.

⚙️ Implementation

Added _precompute_signs_and_sigs() for trace-level precomputation

Updated metrics:

MSI

CPI

TSL

GOS

BTI
to optionally consume precomputed data

Enforced immutability and length checks

Preserved all APIs and execution paths

📊 Measured Impact

For trace length N = 20 (default windows):

_sign() calls: 81 → 20

Reduction: 75.3%

Redundant per-metric recomputation is eliminated; all metrics reuse precomputed values.

🧪 Test Coverage

+20 formal validation tests (90 total)

Covers:

equivalence (value + byte)

mutation safety

window safety

determinism

redundancy measurement

Full test suite passes with no changes to existing assertions

🛡️ Safety Guarantees

No behavioral changes

Bitwise identical outputs

float64 semantics preserved

Deterministic execution maintained

No mutation of cached data

No assumptions about window equality

⚠️ Scope & Limitations

Optimization is per-call only (no cross-call caching)

Applies to metrics using sign/CRC:

MSI, CPI, TSL, GOS, BTI

LEC, CVNE, and EDS are unaffected

🧠 Summary

v68.5.0 extends invariant-driven optimization from algebraic identity (v68.4.1) to data-level reuse. By exploiting trace-indexed deterministic transformations, redundant computation is eliminated while preserving full reproducibility and correctness guarantees.

Formal validation of a trace-indexed invariant enabling elimination of redundant sign and CRC computations in deterministic BP dynamics analysis.
