spectral phase diagrams of Tanner graph stability

title: v7.4.0 — Spectral Tanner Graph Design Rules
body:
Release Notes
v7.4.0 — Spectral Tanner Graph Design Rules

This release introduces spectral Tanner graph design diagnostics that evaluate whether a graph is structurally stable for belief propagation decoding before the decoder runs.

The new module converts spectral diagnostics into deterministic design-rule checks based on graph spectral structure.

New Capabilities

Spectral Design Score

A normalized stability score combining:

NB spectral radius
eigenvector localization (IPR)
cluster risk scores
spectral instability ratio
spectral gap penalty

The score ranges from:

0 → stable graph
1 → high instability risk

Spectral Gap Diagnostics

The framework now computes the spectral gap:

|λ1| − |λ2|

along with the ratio:

|λ2| / |λ1|

Small spectral gaps indicate competing eigenmodes and potential BP instability.

Structural Risk Pattern Detection

The design analysis detects known BP failure signals including:

high eigenvector localization
high-risk spectral clusters
small spectral gap
high instability ratio

Node and cluster lists are returned in deterministic sorted order.

CLI Integration

New CLI flag:

--spectral-design-analysis

Runs graph design diagnostics without executing the decoder.

Tests

30 tests verify:

score bounds
spectral gap computation
structural risk detection
deterministic outputs
JSON stability
decoder safety

Architectural Guarantees

Decoder core unchanged
Schema unchanged
Deterministic outputs preserved
No new dependencies

The feature operates entirely within the experimental layer.

title: v7.3.0 — Deterministic Multi-Step Spectral Graph Repair
body:
Release Notes
v7.3.0 — Deterministic Multi-Step Spectral Graph Repair

This release extends the spectral Tanner graph repair framework with deterministic multi-step search and spectral-bound pruning.

The repair algorithm can now evaluate repair sequences rather than a single edge swap, enabling deeper exploration of structural improvements that reduce predicted belief propagation (BP) instability before decoding begins.

The implementation remains fully deterministic and operates entirely within the experimental layer of the framework.

New Features
Multi-Step Spectral Graph Repair

The repair loop now supports deterministic repair sequences:

baseline graph
 → swap₁
 → swap₂
 → evaluate repaired graph

Search depth is controlled by:

max_repair_depth
enable_multistep_repair

Depth=1 reproduces the original single-repair behavior.

Spectral-Bound Branch Pruning

A deterministic branch-and-bound pruning rule eliminates repair branches that cannot outperform the best repair discovered so far.

This dramatically reduces search complexity while preserving optimality.

Pruning is enabled via:

enable_pruning
Deterministic Repair Sequence Selection

Repair sequences are selected using a stable tie-breaking rule:

1. score improvement (DESC)
2. repaired instability score (ASC)
3. swap1 description (ASC)
4. swap2 description (ASC)

This guarantees identical repair results across repeated runs.

CLI Integration

New CLI flag:

--spectral-multistep-repair

This enables deterministic multi-step repair search during evaluation.

The flag implies:

--spectral-graph-repair-loop
Repair Artifact Extensions

Repair artifacts now include additional metadata describing the repair search:

repair_depth
repair_sequence
sequence_length
multistep_enabled
pruning_enabled
branches_pruned

All outputs remain deterministic and JSON-stable.

Tests

15 new tests verify:

deterministic execution
candidate validity and degree preservation
branch pruning correctness
depth-1 backward compatibility
JSON artifact stability
decoder safety guarantees
repair sequence tie-breaking

All tests pass.

Architectural Guarantees
Decoder core unchanged
Schema unchanged
Determinism preserved
Baseline decoding identical when feature disabled

All repair logic operates entirely within the experimental layer.

Research Context

This release upgrades the spectral graph repair loop into a deterministic search over Tanner graph modifications.
The framework can now explore deeper structural repairs that reduce predicted BP instability before decoding begins.

This capability forms the foundation for upcoming releases:

v7.4.0 — Spectral Tanner Graph Design Rules
v7.5.0 — Predictor-Guided Graph Optimization

title: v7.2.0 — Spectral Instability Phase Map
body:
Release Notes
v7.2.0 — Spectral Instability Phase Map

This release introduces a spectral instability phase map that predicts belief propagation (BP) decoding failure probability directly from Tanner graph spectral diagnostics.

The feature extends the experimental framework introduced in the v7.x series by adding a deterministic structural predictor that estimates decoder instability before BP decoding runs. Prediction accuracy is then evaluated against observed decoding outcomes across the phase diagram.

The implementation remains fully deterministic, opt-in, and decoder-safe. The decoder core is unchanged.

Added
Spectral Instability Phase Map

New experiment module:

src/qec/experiments/spectral_instability_phase_map.py

The phase map compares predicted decoding instability against observed BP decoding outcomes for each trial.

The instability predictor combines multiple structural signals derived from spectral diagnostics:

normalized NB spectral radius
eigenvector localization (IPR)
maximum trapping-set cluster risk
spectral instability ratio

These signals are combined into a deterministic instability score:

spectral_instability_score ∈ [0,1]

The score estimates the likelihood that BP decoding will fail for the given Tanner graph and noise realization.

Prediction Evaluation

Each trial records:

spectral_instability_score
predicted_instability
observed_failure
prediction_correct

Prediction accuracy can therefore be evaluated across different error regimes and code parameters.

ROC Curve and AUC Metrics

This release adds deterministic ROC analysis for evaluating predictor performance.

New metrics:

roc_auc
roc_curve_fpr
roc_curve_tpr
roc_curve_thresholds

The ROC curve is constructed using sorted unique instability scores as thresholds, ensuring deterministic ordering.

The AUC (Area Under Curve) is computed via deterministic trapezoidal integration and rounded to 12 decimal places.

These metrics enable quantitative evaluation of the spectral instability predictor.

Phase Diagram Metrics

The benchmark harness aggregates the following prediction statistics:

mean_spectral_instability_score
prediction_accuracy
false_positive_rate
false_negative_rate
predicted_failure_fraction
observed_failure_fraction
roc_auc

These metrics allow analysis of predictor performance across the decoding phase diagram.

CLI Integration

New flag:

--spectral-phase-map

Enabling this flag activates the phase map experiment pipeline:

spectral diagnostics
→ BP instability predictor
→ baseline decoding
→ prediction evaluation

Default benchmark behavior remains unchanged when the flag is not used.

Tests

New test coverage added in:

tests/test_spectral_phase_map.py

53 tests total across the phase map module and ROC/AUC patch.

Test coverage includes:

Predictor Validation

instability score bounds

deterministic scoring

predictor consistency

ROC Curve Validation

deterministic ROC construction

sorted thresholds

monotonic TPR/FPR

rounding behavior

AUC Validation

bounds checking (0 ≤ AUC ≤ 1)

perfect predictor case (AUC = 1.0)

random predictor baseline (~0.5)

Pipeline Integration

full stack execution from spectral diagnostics through decoding

prediction vs outcome evaluation

Determinism

repeated runs produce identical artifacts

Decoder Safety

no imports from decoder modules

baseline decoding unchanged when the experiment is disabled

All tests passing.

Determinism Guarantees

This release preserves all determinism invariants of the framework.

Specifically:

no randomness introduced

deterministic ROC threshold ordering

stable sorting for all collections

pure functions for predictor calculations

float outputs rounded to 12 decimals

Repeated runs produce byte-identical artifacts.

Decoder Safety

The decoder core remains untouched.

No files inside:

src/qec/decoder/

were modified.

All prediction logic operates outside the decoder, and decoding is invoked exactly as existing benchmark code does.

Baseline decoding behavior remains byte-identical when the phase map experiment is not enabled.

Dependencies

No dependency changes.

The project continues to rely only on:

Python stdlib
NumPy
Compatibility

decoder core unchanged

schema unchanged

artifact format unchanged

backward compatible with v7.0 and v7.1 experiments

Summary

v7.2 introduces a deterministic spectral instability predictor and phase map experiment that evaluates the relationship between Tanner graph spectral structure and BP decoding failure.

With the addition of ROC and AUC evaluation metrics, the framework now supports quantitative assessment of structural predictors of decoding instability, further strengthening QEC as a deterministic research platform for studying belief propagation dynamics on QLDPC Tanner graphs.

title: v7.1.0 — Cluster-Aware Spectral Decoder Control
body:
Release Notes
v7.1.0 — Cluster-Aware Spectral Decoder Control

This release extends the Spectral-Guided Decoder Control Framework (v7.0) with a new experimental scheduling strategy that prioritizes nodes belonging to high-risk spectral trapping-set clusters.

The feature enables deterministic experiments that test whether prioritizing updates for nodes inside unstable Tanner-graph regions improves belief propagation convergence and decoding stability.

The implementation remains fully deterministic, opt-in, and decoder-safe.

The decoder core is unchanged.

Added
Cluster-Aware Decoder Control

New controller mode:

risk_cluster_schedule

This scheduling mode prioritizes nodes belonging to the highest-risk spectral cluster identified by spectral diagnostics and trapping-set analysis.

Cluster nodes are updated first during the variable-to-check (V2C) update phase in a deterministic two-phase scheduling order.

Update ordering:

cluster_nodes → remaining_nodes

This allows targeted experiments on whether spectral trapping-set regions benefit from prioritized message updates.

Implementation components:

_extract_cluster_nodes()
_experimental_bp_cluster_scheduled()
CONTROL_MODE_RISK_CLUSTER_SCHEDULE

Cluster nodes are deterministically extracted from:

top_risk_clusters
candidate_clusters

All node orderings are stable and deterministic.

Controller Artifact Fields

The controller experiment output now includes additional cluster diagnostics:

cluster_control_enabled
cluster_nodes
cluster_size
cluster_risk_score
cluster_priority_fraction

All fields are JSON-serializable and float values are rounded to 12 decimals for artifact stability.

CLI Integration

New flag:

--spectral-cluster-control

Enabling this flag automatically activates:

--spectral-decoder-controller
--bp-stability-predictor

Execution pipeline:

spectral diagnostics
→ BP instability predictor
→ cluster-aware decoder controller
→ controlled decoding experiment

Default behavior remains unchanged when the flag is not used.

Phase Diagram Metrics

The benchmark harness now aggregates cluster scheduling statistics:

mean_cluster_size
mean_cluster_priority_fraction
cluster_schedule_activation_rate

These metrics allow phase diagram analysis of when cluster scheduling is activated and how large prioritized clusters are across error regimes.

Tests

Cluster Extraction

deterministic ordering

reproducibility

Cluster-Scheduled BP

deterministic behavior

empty cluster fallback

valid decoder outputs

fallback to standard mode

repeated runs produce identical artifacts

cluster nodes remain sorted

JSON Artifact Stability

serialization round-trip

correct field types

float precision validation

Decoder Safety

no decoder imports

baseline decoding unchanged when controller disabled

cluster metadata does not affect baseline decode

Pipeline Integration

full stack execution from spectral diagnostics through predictor and controller

All tests passing.

Determinism Guarantees

This release preserves the determinism invariants of the framework.

Specifically:

no randomness introduced

stable node ordering via sorted()

no reliance on dictionary iteration order

deterministic cluster extraction

float outputs rounded to 12 decimals

controller logic implemented as pure functions of inputs

Repeated runs produce byte-identical artifacts.

Decoder Safety

The decoder core remains untouched.

No files inside:

src/qec/decoder/

were modified.

The cluster scheduling experiment operates entirely through external experimental decoding wrappers.

Baseline decoding remains bit-identical when cluster control is disabled.

Architecture

This release strengthens the closed-loop experimental decoding architecture introduced in v7.0:

Tanner graph
      ↓
spectral diagnostics
      ↓
BP instability predictor
      ↓
decoder controller
      ↓
controlled decoding experiment

Cluster-aware scheduling provides the first structure-aware decoding policy built on spectral trapping-set diagnostics.

Dependencies

No dependency changes.

The project continues to rely on:

Python stdlib
NumPy
Compatibility

decoder core unchanged

schema unchanged

artifact format unchanged

backward compatible with all v7.0 experiments

Summary

v7.1 introduces cluster-aware scheduling experiments that prioritize nodes inside spectral trapping-set regions while preserving the strict determinism and architectural safety guarantees of the QEC framework.

The release expands the experimental control layer without altering decoder semantics, enabling deeper investigation of the relationship between Tanner-graph spectral structure and belief propagation stability.

title: v7.0.0 — Spectral-Guided Decoder Control Framework
body:
Release Notes
v7.0.0 — Spectral-Guided Decoder Control Framework

This release introduces the first spectral-guided decoder control layer for the QSOL Quantum Error Correction toolkit.

The new framework integrates spectral diagnostics and BP stability prediction into a deterministic experimental controller capable of steering decoder behavior without modifying the core decoding algorithms.

The controller operates as an external policy layer, preserving the architecture and determinism of the decoder while enabling new research into instability-aware decoding strategies.

Highlights

Spectral Decoder Control Layer

New experimental module:

src/qec/experiments/spectral_decoder_controller.py

Primary entry point:

run_spectral_decoder_control_experiment(...)

This module consumes spectral diagnostics and BP stability predictor outputs to determine decoder control policies during experiments.

Predictor-Guided Scheduling

Decoder execution can now adapt based on predicted instability signals.

Control modes:

standard
risk_guided_damping
risk_guided_schedule

These modes allow controlled experiments exploring how spectral signals influence decoding stability.

Adaptive Damping

Implements deterministic per-node damping derived from node risk scores:

damping(node) = base_damping + alpha * normalized_risk

Default parameters:

base_damping = 0.5
alpha = 0.25

Damping values are safely constrained:

0.5 ≤ damping ≤ 0.9

This helps stabilize message oscillations in high-risk Tanner graph regions.

Decoder Mode Switching Hook

v7.0 introduces a clean interface for future adaptive decoding strategies.

Potential future transitions:

BP → OSD
BP → BPGD
BP → hybrid decoders

The hook is implemented but no additional decoders are introduced in this release.

CLI Integration

New CLI flag:

--spectral-decoder-controller

Activates the full experimental pipeline:

spectral diagnostics
→ BP stability predictor
→ spectral decoder controller
→ controlled decoding experiment

The feature is optional and does not affect existing experiments when disabled.

Phase Diagram Extensions

Phase diagram experiments now include controller-specific metrics:

mean_controller_bp_failure_risk
controller_instability_fraction
mean_controller_accuracy
mean_controller_error_rate
mean_controlled_delta_iterations
mean_controlled_delta_success

These metrics enable quantitative evaluation of spectral-guided control effects on decoding performance.

Determinism Guarantees

The v7.0 controller preserves all framework invariants:

No randomness introduced

Deterministic node ordering

Stable JSON output artifacts

No modifications to core decoder implementations

Fully additive experimental infrastructure

Testing

New test suite:

tests/test_spectral_decoder_controller.py

Test coverage includes:

controller determinism

control-mode selection

damping bounds enforcement

JSON artifact serialization

pipeline integration

decoder safety verification

Repository Improvements

Added permanent repository file:

AUDIT_CHECKLIST.md

This document defines the standard pre-merge audit procedure used to verify:

decoder integrity

determinism

experimental isolation

numerical safety

CLI compatibility

artifact stability

Architectural Milestone

v7.0 establishes a new experimental control stack:

spectral diagnostics
        ↓
BP stability predictor
        ↓
spectral decoder controller
        ↓
decoder execution
        ↓
experimental outcomes

This transforms the QSOL QEC framework into a closed-loop spectral-aware decoding research platform.

Future Work

Planned extensions to the controller framework include:

cluster-aware scheduling policies

spectral-localization damping profiles

adaptive decoder switching strategies

predictive stability maps for Tanner graph design

Release Notes
Highlights

Improves performance of spectral graph optimization experiments introduced in v6.7.0.

Spectral radius computation for the non-backtracking matrix now uses deterministic power iteration instead of full eigendecomposition.

Changes

title: v6.7.0 — Spectral Graph Optimization
body:
Release Notes
Highlights

Introduces spectral graph optimization for Tanner graph repair experiments.
Candidate structural swaps are now evaluated using the spectral radius of the non-backtracking (Hashimoto) matrix as an objective, enabling direct optimization of graph structure for improved belief propagation stability.

The implementation performs incremental spectral evaluation, allowing candidate swaps to be scored without rebuilding the full NB matrix for every candidate.

Added

Incremental Spectral Swap Evaluation

_spectral_score_with_swap(...)

Reuses a base NB matrix and updates only the rows/columns affected by the swapped edges

Avoids rebuilding the full NB matrix for every candidate swap

Spectral scores verified to be bit-identical with the full recomputation method

NB Context Infrastructure

_build_nb_context(edges)

Builds reusable NB matrix representation

Returns:

NB matrix

directed edge list

non-backtracking adjacency map

edge index mapping

This structure allows the spectral machinery to be reused across experiments.

Spectral Radius Helper

_spectral_radius_from_nb(B)

Extracted spectral radius computation into a shared helper

Provides a consistent entry point for spectral evaluation across experiments

Design

The spectral optimization experiment now follows this pipeline:

Tanner graph
→ construct NB matrix
→ generate candidate edge swaps
→ incrementally update NB rows/columns
→ compute spectral radius
→ select best structural repair

This allows structural rewrites to be evaluated efficiently without modifying the decoder.

Performance

The NB matrix is now constructed once per graph and reused across swap evaluations.

This significantly reduces the cost of spectral scoring compared to rebuilding the NB matrix for every candidate.

Further spectral computation optimizations will be introduced in v6.7.1.

Testing

6 new tests covering incremental NB updates

Spectral scores verified to match full recomputation

1715 tests passing, 0 failures

Compatibility

Decoder core unchanged

JSON schema unchanged

Public APIs unchanged

Fully deterministic outputs maintained

title: v6.6.0 — Tanner Graph Fragility Repair
body:
Release Notes
v6.6.0 — Tanner Graph Fragility Repair

Deterministic Structural Repair Experiments for Fragile Tanner Graph Regions

This release introduces the first Tanner graph repair experiment in the QSOL QEC framework.
Using spectral diagnostics and structural risk signals developed in earlier releases, the system can now attempt deterministic structural modifications to fragile graph regions and measure the resulting decoder behavior.

Candidate edge swaps are generated near high-risk clusters and evaluated using a structural repair score. The best candidate swap is applied to a temporary graph copy, and baseline vs repaired decoder runs are compared.

This marks the first stage where the framework moves beyond structural analysis into structural repair experiments.

Added
Tanner Graph Repair Experiment

New experimental module:

src/qec/experiments/tanner_graph_repair.py

The experiment attempts to disrupt fragile Tanner graph motifs through deterministic edge swaps.

Edge swaps preserve node degrees and bipartite structure:

(v1,c1) + (v2,c2)
→
(v1,c2) + (v2,c1)

Constraints enforced:

bipartite structure preserved

node degrees unchanged

duplicate edges prevented

deterministic candidate selection

Candidate Swap Search Optimization

Candidate swaps are generated using a cluster-local search strategy:

cluster_edges × boundary_edges

Where:

cluster_edges  = edges touching fragile cluster nodes
boundary_edges = edges touching neighbors of the cluster

This dramatically reduces the candidate search space compared to scanning all edges in the Tanner graph.

Structural Repair Scoring

Candidate swaps are evaluated using a deterministic repair metric:

repair_score(graph, cluster)

The default implementation counts edges whose endpoints both remain inside the fragile cluster. Lower scores indicate stronger disruption of the fragile structure.

The scoring function is implemented as a separate interface to support future spectral objectives.

CLI Integration

New optional flag:

--tanner-graph-repair-experiment

When enabled, the evaluation harness will:

compute spectral diagnostics

compute structural risk scores

identify fragile clusters

generate candidate edge swaps

select the best repair

run baseline vs repaired decoder experiments

Test Coverage

New test suite:

tests/test_tanner_graph_repair.py

26 new tests covering:

deterministic candidate swap generation

node degree preservation

duplicate edge prevention

cluster-local candidate search

repair score correctness

JSON serialization stability

experiment output validation

end-to-end pipeline integration

Total test results:

1676 passed
7 skipped
0 failed
Limitations

Cluster membership is inferred from node_risk_scores rather than the original v6.2 clustering stage

Repair scoring currently uses a simple structural metric

Candidate swap generation is bounded by max_candidates to control runtime

Experimental BP loops remain separate from the production decoder implementation

Research Context

The v6 series now forms a layered spectral analysis and experimentation stack:

v6.0  spectral stability diagnostics
v6.1  spectral localization
v6.2  spectral trapping-set candidate detection
v6.3  spectral–BP attractor alignment
v6.4  spectral failure risk scoring
v6.5  risk-aware decoder experiments
v6.6  Tanner graph fragility repair

Together these components allow the framework to:

detect fragile Tanner graph regions
→ attempt deterministic structural repair
→ measure decoder behavior
Next Direction

Future releases will introduce spectral graph optimization, replacing the structural repair score with a spectral objective:

minimize ρ(B)

where

B = non-backtracking matrix
ρ(B) = spectral radius

Reducing the non-backtracking spectral radius is strongly associated with improved belief propagation stability.

This direction will form the basis of v6.7 — Spectral Graph Optimization.

Philosophy

Small is beautiful.
Determinism is holy.
Structure is signal.

title: v6.5.0 — Risk-Aware Decoder Experiments
body:
Release Notes
v6.5.0 — Risk-Aware Decoder Experiments

Exploring Decoder Control Using Spectral Risk Signals

This release introduces experimental decoding strategies that use structural risk signals derived from spectral diagnostics to guide belief propagation (BP) behavior.

Building on the spectral analysis stack developed in earlier releases, v6.5 tests whether fragile regions of the Tanner graph—identified through spectral localization and BP activity alignment—can be used to influence decoder dynamics.

Two deterministic experiments are introduced: risk-aware damping and risk-guided perturbation. These experiments explore whether targeted interventions near high-risk nodes can stabilize BP updates or help the decoder escape stalled attractor states.

All functionality is strictly experimental and additive. The production BP decoder implementation remains unchanged.

Added
Risk-Aware Damping Experiment

Introduces a decoder experiment that increases message damping for nodes with elevated structural risk.

High-risk nodes are identified using:

node_risk_score ≥ 0.5 × max(node_risk_scores)

The experiment runs two deterministic decodes:

baseline decode
risk-aware damped decode

Damping for high-risk nodes is increased by a fixed factor while other nodes retain the baseline damping parameter.

Comparison metrics include:

delta_iterations
delta_success
residual_norms
Risk-Guided Perturbation Experiment

Adds an experiment that applies a deterministic perturbation when belief propagation stalls.

A stall condition is detected when message residual change falls below a threshold for a configurable iteration window.

When a stall occurs, a small perturbation is applied to high-risk nodes:

llr[node] += 0.1 × mean(|llr|) × sign(llr[node])

Decoding then continues, allowing the experiment to test whether fragile regions can be nudged out of metastable states.

Reported metrics include:

stall_detected
perturbation_applied
iterations_after_perturbation
delta_iterations
delta_success
CLI Integration

Two new optional experiment flags are introduced:

--risk-aware-damping-experiment
--risk-guided-perturbation-experiment

Both experiments automatically enable the structural diagnostics required to compute risk scores.

Structural Diagnostics Pass-Through

Experiment outputs include the structural risk signals produced by the spectral pipeline:

node_risk_scores
cluster_risk_scores
top_risk_clusters

These signals are preserved to support future work on Tanner graph structural optimization.

Research Context

The v6 series introduces a layered spectral analysis framework connecting Tanner graph structure to BP dynamics.

Current spectral stack:

v6.0 — Spectral stability diagnostics
v6.1 — Spectral localization diagnostics
v6.2 — Spectral trapping-set candidate detection
v6.3 — Spectral–BP attractor alignment
v6.4 — Spectral failure risk scoring
v6.5 — Risk-aware decoder experiments

Together these components allow the framework to detect fragile graph structures, measure their influence on decoder dynamics, and experimentally test decoder interventions guided by structural signals.

Next Direction

Future releases will explore structural Tanner graph improvement using the detected risk signals.

Planned work includes deterministic graph rewiring strategies that disrupt fragile motifs while preserving LDPC code structure.

Philosophy

Small is beautiful.
Determinism is holy.
Negative results are data.

title: v6.4.0 — Spectral Failure Risk Scoring
body:
Release Notes
v6.4.0 — Spectral Failure Risk Scoring

Structural Risk Estimation from Spectral Localization and BP Dynamics

This release introduces a deterministic structural risk diagnostic that estimates how strongly candidate Tanner graph substructures may influence belief propagation (BP) decoding dynamics.

Building on the spectral pipeline introduced in earlier releases, v6.4 combines:

spectral localization of non-backtracking eigenmodes (v6.1)

spectral trapping-set candidate detection (v6.2)

spectral–BP attractor alignment (v6.3)

to produce a Spectral Failure Risk Score for candidate clusters.

The risk score integrates structural participation, spectral instability, and observed BP dynamical activity into a single scalar metric that ranks candidate fragile subgraphs.

All diagnostics remain strictly additive and deterministic. The BP decoder implementation remains unchanged.

Added
Spectral Failure Risk Diagnostic

Introduces a new diagnostic module that computes structural risk scores for candidate Tanner graph clusters.

Cluster risk is defined as:

cluster_risk = participation_weight × alignment_score × localization_weight

Where:

participation_weight — mean participation of cluster nodes in localized spectral modes (v6.2)

alignment_score — spectral–BP attractor alignment for the cluster (v6.3)

localization_weight — mean inverse participation ratio (IPR) of contributing spectral modes (v6.1)

The diagnostic outputs:

cluster_risk_scores

node_risk_scores

cluster_risk_ranking

top_risk_clusters

max_cluster_risk

mean_cluster_risk

Node-level risk is computed by aggregating cluster risk contributions across clusters containing each variable node.

CLI Support

Adds a new optional flag:

--spectral-failure-risk

When enabled, the experiment harness computes structural risk scores after spectral localization, trapping-set candidate detection, and spectral–BP alignment diagnostics.

Required upstream diagnostics are automatically enabled.

Phase Diagram Integration

Structural risk statistics integrate with phase-diagram experiments.

New aggregation metrics include:

mean_cluster_risk

max_cluster_risk

mean_num_high_risk_clusters

These allow structural fragility indicators to be studied alongside decoding regimes.

Design Principles

The spectral failure risk score is designed as a deterministic structural heuristic, not a definitive predictor of decoding failure.

Key properties:

deterministic execution

JSON-stable experiment artifacts

no modification of BP decoding behavior

additive diagnostics layered on existing analysis modules

Only variable nodes contribute to node-level risk aggregation, preserving physical interpretation of qubit-level fragility.

Research Context

The v6 series introduces a layered spectral analysis framework linking Tanner graph structure to belief propagation dynamics.

The current spectral stack includes:

v6.0 — Spectral stability diagnostics
v6.1 — Spectral localization diagnostics
v6.2 — Spectral trapping-set candidate detection
v6.3 — Spectral–BP attractor alignment
v6.4 — Spectral failure risk scoring

Together these diagnostics enable systematic investigation of how spectral structure, localized graph motifs, and BP dynamics interact to influence decoding behavior.

Next Direction

Future work will explore risk-aware decoder experiments, testing whether structural risk signals can guide decoder scheduling or damping strategies.

Philosophy

Small is beautiful.
Determinism is holy.
Negative results are data.

title: v6.3.0 — Spectral–BP Attractor Alignment
body:
Short Release Notes

Spectral–BP Attractor Alignment Diagnostics

This release introduces a deterministic diagnostic that measures alignment between spectral trapping-set candidates identified in earlier releases and dynamically active nodes observed during belief propagation (BP) decoding.

Using the belief oscillation index (BOI) as a node-level activity signal, the framework identifies BP-active nodes and computes overlap with spectrally predicted fragile regions. Alignment is quantified using the Jaccard index between candidate nodes and BP-active nodes, with additional cluster-level alignment metrics.

The diagnostic provides the first explicit bridge in the framework between spectral structure and decoder dynamics, enabling experiments that test whether localized spectral modes correspond to regions of elevated BP activity.

All functionality remains strictly additive and deterministic. The BP decoder implementation is unchanged.

Added

spectral_bp_alignment diagnostic module

alignment metrics between spectral candidates and BP-active nodes

cluster-level alignment scores

CLI flag --spectral-bp-alignment

phase diagram aggregation metrics for alignment statistics

This release completes the initial spectral analysis stack:

v6.0 — Spectral stability diagnostics  
v6.1 — Spectral localization diagnostics  
v6.2 — Spectral trapping-set candidate detection  
v6.3 — Spectral–BP attractor alignment

These tools enable systematic investigation of the relationship between Tanner graph structure, spectral behavior, and belief propagation dynamics.

title: v6.2.0 — Spectral Trapping-Set Candidate Detection
body:
Release Notes
v6.2.0 — Spectral Trapping-Set Candidate Detection

Structural Detection of Candidate Fragile Subgraphs from Spectral Localization

This release introduces a deterministic diagnostic for identifying candidate fragile subgraphs in Tanner graphs using the spectral localization results introduced in v6.1.

The diagnostic analyzes participation of variable and check nodes across localized non-backtracking eigenmodes. Nodes that repeatedly appear in multiple localized modes are flagged as structural candidates for trapping-set-like regions.

Candidate nodes are grouped into clusters using Tanner graph connectivity, producing interpretable subgraphs that may influence belief propagation (BP) decoding dynamics.

All functionality remains strictly additive and deterministic. The BP decoder implementation is unchanged.

Added
Spectral Trapping-Set Candidate Detection

Introduces a deterministic diagnostic for identifying Tanner graph regions that repeatedly participate in localized spectral modes.

The algorithm operates in five steps:

Iterate through localized non-backtracking eigenmodes detected by v6.1.

Count node participation across modes.

Flag nodes whose participation count exceeds a configurable threshold.

Construct candidate clusters using Tanner graph connectivity.

Report participation statistics and cluster structure.

Outputs include:

node_participation_counts

candidate_variable_nodes

candidate_check_nodes

candidate_clusters

max_node_participation

These diagnostics provide a structural probe for fragile graph regions that may correspond to trapping sets or absorbing sets.

Cluster Detection

Candidate nodes are grouped using Tanner graph adjacency and connected-component detection.

Cluster outputs include:

candidate_clusters

cluster_sizes

variable and check node membership

This allows candidate fragile regions to be interpreted as concrete Tanner graph substructures.

Phase Diagram Integration

Spectral trapping-set diagnostics integrate with phase-diagram experiments.

New aggregated metrics include:

mean_nb_candidate_nodes

max_nb_node_participation

num_candidate_clusters

These allow structural fragility indicators to be studied alongside decoder phase-diagram regimes.

CLI Support

Adds a new optional flag:

--nb-trapping-candidates

When enabled, the experiment harness computes trapping-set candidate diagnostics using localization results produced by v6.1.

Design Choices
Participation Threshold

Nodes are considered candidates when they appear in two or more localized spectral modes.

This rule filters isolated spectral artifacts while highlighting nodes that participate in multiple localized instability directions.

Clustering Strategy

Clusters are constructed using Tanner graph connectivity. Nodes sharing graph adjacency are grouped into connected components.

This approach naturally captures small subgraph motifs often associated with decoder failure structures.

Determinism

All diagnostics preserve strict determinism:

no randomness

stable ordering

JSON-stable outputs

reproducible experiment artifacts

The BP decoder implementation remains unchanged.

Testing

v6.2 introduces new validation coverage including:

participation counting tests

cluster detection on toy Tanner graphs

deterministic output verification

JSON serialization stability

integration tests with v6.1 localization outputs

phase diagram smoke tests

All existing tests continue to pass.

Research Context

Non-backtracking spectral operators are closely related to the linearized stability conditions of belief propagation.

Localization of these spectral modes can indicate structural irregularities in Tanner graphs. The diagnostics introduced in v6.2 identify nodes that repeatedly participate in localized spectral structures, producing candidate fragile subgraphs.

These tools extend the spectral analysis stack developed in previous releases:

v5.x — Attractor geometry and BP dynamics
v6.0 — Spectral stability diagnostics
v6.1 — Spectral localization diagnostics
v6.2 — Spectral trapping-set candidate detection

Together these layers enable systematic investigation of the relationship between Tanner graph structure, spectral behavior, and decoding performance.

Next Direction

Future work will investigate the relationship between candidate clusters and observed decoding failures.

This includes measuring alignment between spectral structures and belief propagation trajectories during decoding.

Philosophy

Small is beautiful.
Determinism is holy.
Negative results are data.

title: v6.1.0 — Non-Backtracking Localization
body:
Release Notes
v6.1.0 — Non-Backtracking Localization

Spectral Localization Diagnostics for Tanner Graph Structure

This release introduces deterministic localization diagnostics for the non-backtracking spectrum of Tanner graphs. Using the inverse participation ratio (IPR), the framework now identifies whether leading spectral modes are diffuse across the graph or concentrated on small structural regions.

Localized modes are projected back onto Tanner graph nodes, allowing concentrated spectral structures to be interpreted directly in terms of variable-node and check-node participation. These diagnostics provide a structural lens for studying fragile subgraphs that may influence belief propagation (BP) decoding behavior.

All new functionality is strictly additive and preserves the deterministic experimental guarantees of the framework. The BP decoder implementation remains unchanged.

The localization layer builds directly on the spectral stability diagnostics introduced in v6.0.

Added
Non-Backtracking Localization Diagnostics

Introduces deterministic localization analysis for non-backtracking eigenmodes.

Localization is measured using the inverse participation ratio:

IPR(v) = Σ |vᵢ|⁴ / (Σ |vᵢ|²)²

Key properties:

scale-invariant

supports complex eigenvectors

deterministic ordering

robust identification of concentrated spectral modes

Diagnostics include:

ipr_scores

max_ipr

localized_modes

mode_support_sizes

top_localization_score

These metrics quantify whether spectral modes are broadly distributed across the graph or concentrated on small structural regions.

Directed-Edge Support Projection

Because the non-backtracking operator acts on directed edges, localized spectral support is mapped back to Tanner graph nodes.

Outputs include:

localized_variable_nodes

localized_check_nodes

per_mode_mass_on_variables

per_mode_mass_on_checks

This projection allows localized spectral modes to be interpreted as concrete Tanner graph substructures.

Phase Diagram Integration

Localization diagnostics are integrated into phase-diagram experiments.

Aggregated metrics now include:

mean_nb_max_ipr

mean_nb_num_localized_modes

mean_nb_top_localization_score

These statistics allow decoding regimes to be studied alongside structural spectral localization.

CLI Support

Adds an optional CLI flag:

--nb-localization

This enables non-backtracking localization diagnostics during experiment runs without affecting existing workflows.

Design Choices
Mode Selection

Leading non-backtracking modes are analyzed using a deterministic top-k eigenvalue magnitude policy (default k = 6), consistent with v6.0 spectral ordering.

Localization Rule

Spectral support is defined using a deterministic relative threshold:

|vᵢ|² ≥ 0.1 · max(|v|²)

This simple rule reliably identifies concentrated spectral regions without introducing stochastic heuristics.

Localization Threshold

Modes are classified as localized when:

IPR ≥ 2 / (number of directed edges)

This threshold corresponds to approximately twice the uniform mass distribution.

Testing

v6.1 adds extensive validation coverage:

IPR correctness tests

complex eigenvector handling

scale invariance checks

deterministic output verification

JSON serialization stability

directed-edge → node projection tests

phase-diagram integration smoke tests

All tests pass with the existing framework.

Research Context

Non-backtracking spectral operators are closely related to the stability conditions of belief propagation. Localization of leading spectral modes can indicate the presence of small structural defects or fragile subgraphs in Tanner graphs.

The diagnostics introduced in v6.1 provide a deterministic structural probe for investigating such phenomena.

These tools extend the experimental stack introduced in earlier releases:

v5.x — Attractor geometry and BP dynamics
v6.0 — Spectral stability diagnostics
v6.1 — Spectral localization diagnostics

Together these layers support a unified research program linking Tanner graph structure, spectral properties, BP dynamics, and decoder failure regimes.

Next Direction

Future work will investigate whether localized spectral modes correlate with observed decoding failures.

If validated, these diagnostics may support structural predictors of fragile decoding regions in QLDPC Tanner graphs.

Philosophy

Small is beautiful.
Determinism is holy.
Negative results are data.

title: v6.0.0 — Spectral Stability Diagnostics
body:
Release Notes
v6.0.0 — Spectral Stability Diagnostics

Structural Proxies for Belief Propagation Stability

This release introduces a new diagnostics layer that connects Tanner graph structure with theoretical stability conditions for belief propagation (BP) decoding.

The framework now includes spectral tools that approximate the stability of BP fixed points using graph-theoretic operators such as the non-backtracking matrix and Bethe Hessian.

These diagnostics extend the existing experimental stack — attractor geometry analysis, spectral Tanner graph diagnostics, BP dynamical exploration, ternary topology classification, and empirical phase diagrams — with theoretical stability proxies.

All diagnostics remain strictly deterministic and non-intrusive. The BP decoder itself is unchanged and continues to function as a frozen experimental baseline.

The result is a unified experimental framework capable of studying relationships between:

Tanner graph topology
↓
spectral stability indicators
↓
BP dynamical behavior
↓
decoder failure regimes

Added
Non-Backtracking Spectrum Analysis

Introduces computation of the non-backtracking (Hashimoto) operator spectrum for Tanner graphs.

The non-backtracking spectrum provides insight into the stability of BP dynamics and reveals structural fragility that may contribute to decoding failures.

Diagnostics include:

leading non-backtracking eigenvalues

spectral radius estimates

structural stability indicators

These metrics help identify Tanner graphs whose topology may induce unstable BP fixed points.

Bethe Hessian Spectral Analysis

Adds support for computing the Bethe Hessian spectrum, a graph operator closely related to theoretical BP stability conditions.

The Bethe Hessian provides a tractable approximation to stability criteria derived from statistical physics formulations of belief propagation.

Outputs include:

Bethe Hessian eigenvalues

minimum eigenvalue indicators

stability proxy metrics

Small or negative eigenvalues can signal structural instability in the decoding landscape.

BP Stability Proxy

Introduces an empirical BP stability score derived from spectral quantities.

This metric acts as a heuristic indicator of whether decoding is operating in a stable or fragile regime.

The score is intended as a research diagnostic, not a formal proof of stability.

BP Jacobian Spectral Radius Estimator

Adds a deterministic estimator for the spectral radius of the BP update Jacobian.

The Jacobian spectral radius approximates whether small perturbations grow or contract under BP iterations.

A spectral radius below 1 suggests local stability of the BP fixed point.

The estimator operates purely through iteration-trajectory diagnostics, ensuring the BP decoder implementation remains untouched.

Phase Diagram Integration

Spectral stability metrics are now integrated into deterministic phase-diagram experiments.

Each grid cell can aggregate statistics including:

mean spectral radius

mean Bethe Hessian minimum eigenvalue

mean BP stability score

mean Jacobian spectral radius estimate

This allows empirical decoding regimes to be analyzed alongside theoretical stability indicators.

Design Guarantees

As with all diagnostics in the framework, the spectral tools follow strict architectural constraints:

deterministic execution

no randomness or stochastic sampling

JSON-stable experiment artifacts

decoder implementation unchanged

diagnostics operate outside the decoding core

The BP decoder remains a stable experimental object, ensuring that all historical experiments remain reproducible.

Research Impact

v6.0 represents a transition from purely empirical decoder diagnostics toward structural stability analysis of QLDPC decoding.

The framework now contains complementary analysis layers:

Tanner graph spectral diagnostics

attractor geometry analysis

BP dynamical diagnostics

ternary topology classification

empirical decoder phase diagrams

spectral stability diagnostics

Together these tools allow researchers to investigate how graph structure influences decoder behavior and failure modes.

Next Direction (v6.1)

The next stage will explore localization in the non-backtracking spectrum using inverse participation ratio (IPR) metrics.

High-IPR eigenvectors indicate localized graph structures.

Hypothesis:

localized non-backtracking eigenvectors correspond to fragile subgraphs such as trapping sets.

Future diagnostics may therefore enable predicting decoding fragility directly from Tanner graph structure before running BP.

Philosophy

Small is beautiful.
Determinism is holy.
Negative results are data.

title: v5.6.0 — Spectral Trapping-Set Diagnostics
body:
Release Notes
Overview

This release introduces Spectral Trapping-Set Diagnostics, extending the v5 diagnostic framework for analyzing structural fragility in QLDPC Tanner graphs.

The new diagnostic identifies localized spectral clusters in Tanner graph eigenvectors that may correspond to trapping sets or pseudocodeword-prone regions. These clusters represent small subsets of variable nodes where belief propagation (BP) messages may become trapped, potentially contributing to decoding failures or elevated error floors.

Unlike many trapping-set detection approaches, this diagnostic operates purely on graph spectral structure and does not require running BP decoding.

Added
Spectral Trapping-Set Diagnostic

New module:

src/qec/diagnostics/spectral_trapping_sets.py

Function:

compute_spectral_trapping_sets(
    spectral_modes,
    variable_node_count
)

For each spectral eigenvector, the diagnostic:

Extracts variable-node components

Normalizes the vector magnitude

Computes node importance:

importance[i] = |v[i]|

Applies a deterministic localization threshold:

threshold = mean(importance) + std(importance)

Identifies nodes exceeding the threshold as members of a localized spectral cluster.

Output Metrics

The diagnostic returns JSON-serializable results:

cluster_count
largest_cluster_size
mean_cluster_size
clusters

Each cluster record includes:

mode_index
cluster_size
nodes
max_importance
mean_importance

These metrics allow correlation studies between spectral localization and decoding behavior.

Benchmark Harness Integration

The deterministic evaluation harness now supports:

--spectral-trapping-sets

When enabled, the harness:

Runs Tanner spectral analysis (v5.4)

Extracts spectral eigenvectors

Identifies localized spectral clusters

Results are recorded per code instance alongside existing diagnostics.

Performance Improvements

The v5.6 implementation avoids redundant spectral computations.

Previously, the trapping-set analysis rebuilt the Tanner adjacency matrix and performed an additional eigen-decomposition. The harness now reuses eigenvectors produced by the v5.4 Tanner spectral analysis, eliminating an unnecessary O((m+n)^3) computation per code instance.

Additional micro-optimizations include vectorized cluster extraction using NumPy operations.

Tests

New test suite:

tests/test_spectral_trapping_sets.py

Coverage includes:

localized cluster detection

uniform and zero eigenvector handling

deterministic repeated execution

JSON serialization stability

input validation

output structure verification

input mutation protection

All tests pass with deterministic outputs.

Design Principles

The diagnostic follows the core architectural rules of the QEC framework:

deterministic execution

observational diagnostics only

no modification of decoder internals

JSON-stable experiment outputs

The algorithm is purely structural and operates entirely on precomputed spectral modes.

v5 Diagnostic Stack

The v5 series now includes:

v5.1 — BP attractor barrier analysis
v5.3 — BP decision boundary estimation
v5.4 — Tanner spectral diagnostics
v5.5 — Spectral–Boundary Alignment
v5.6 — Spectral Trapping-Set Diagnostics

Together these diagnostics provide a deterministic framework for studying the relationship between:

Tanner graph structure
↓
spectral localization
↓
decision boundary geometry
↓
attractor escape barriers
↓
decoder failure behavior
Research Goal

The spectral trapping-set diagnostic enables investigation of the hypothesis that localized spectral modes correspond to structurally fragile regions of Tanner graphs that contribute to decoding failures.

By correlating spectral clusters with FER scaling and other diagnostics, researchers can explore structural predictors of decoder instability in QLDPC codes.

title: v5.5.0 — Spectral–Boundary Alignment Diagnostics
body:
Release Notes
Overview

This release introduces Spectral–Boundary Alignment Diagnostics, extending the v5 research framework for analyzing the geometry of belief propagation (BP) decoding in QLDPC codes.

The new diagnostic measures the cosine alignment between localized Tanner graph spectral modes and BP decision boundary directions, enabling investigation of whether structural spectral modes correspond to fragile decoding directions.

This feature integrates with the deterministic benchmark harness and records alignment metrics alongside existing boundary and barrier diagnostics.

Added
Spectral–Boundary Alignment Diagnostic

New module:

src/qec/diagnostics/spectral_boundary_alignment.py

Function:

compute_spectral_boundary_alignment(
    spectral_modes,
    boundary_direction
)

Computes cosine similarity between spectral eigenvectors and BP boundary directions:

alignment = |v · d| / (||v|| ||d||)

Outputs:

alignment_scores

dominant_alignment_mode
mode_count

Properties:

deterministic

observational

independent of decoder logic

FER

This enables correlation analysis between spectral structure, BP boundary geometry, and decoding failure rates.

Research Documentation

The report describes:

Tests

All tests pass.

Design Principles

observational diagnostics

no modification of decoder internals

JSON-stable experiment outputs

The feature operates purely as a research diagnostic for studying BP decoding geometry.

v5 Diagnostic Stack

The v5 series now includes four complementary diagnostics:

v5.1 — BP attractor barrier analysis
v5.3 — BP decision boundary estimation
v5.4 — Tanner spectral diagnostics
v5.5 — spectral–boundary alignment

Together these form a deterministic framework for analyzing the relationship between:

Tanner graph structure
↓
spectral localization
↓
decision boundary geometry
↓
attractor escape barriers
↓
decoder failure behavior
Research Goal

The alignment metric allows testing the hypothesis:

localized Tanner spectral modes
≈
fragile BP decision boundary directions

If strong correlations with FER are observed, spectral analysis may provide structural predictors of decoder fragility in QLDPC decoding.

title: v5.4.0 — Tanner Spectral Fragility Diagnostics
body:
Release Notes
v5.4.0 — Tanner Spectral Fragility Diagnostics

This release introduces deterministic spectral diagnostics for Tanner graphs, enabling structural analysis of parity-check matrices through global spectral metrics and localized eigenmode analysis.

The diagnostic measures both global connectivity properties and localized spectral modes of the Tanner graph, providing structural indicators that may correlate with decoding fragility and pseudocodeword-prone regions.

Unlike previous diagnostics in the v5.x series, this analysis operates purely on the graph structure and does not run belief propagation decoding.

Added
Tanner Spectral Analysis Diagnostic

New module:

src/qec/diagnostics/tanner_spectral_analysis.py

Main API:

compute_tanner_spectral_analysis(...)

The diagnostic constructs the bipartite Tanner adjacency matrix from the parity-check matrix and computes:

largest_eigenvalue
adjacency_spectral_gap
laplacian_second_eigenvalue
spectral_ratio

These quantities provide deterministic measurements of global Tanner graph connectivity and expansion quality.

Eigenmode Localization Metrics

For the leading spectral modes of the Tanner adjacency matrix, the diagnostic computes Inverse Participation Ratio (IPR):

IPR(v) = Σ v_i^4

which measures how concentrated an eigenvector is across the graph.

Low IPR values indicate delocalized modes, while higher values indicate localized spectral modes concentrated on a small subset of nodes.

Localization is measured for both:

mode_iprs
variable_mode_iprs

allowing detection of eigenmodes concentrated specifically on variable nodes.

Localized Spectral Node Mapping

The diagnostic identifies the variable nodes contributing most strongly to the most localized eigenmode.

Outputs include:

localized_variable_nodes
localized_variable_weights
localized_variable_fraction

localized_variable_fraction measures the proportion of spectral mass contained within the top localized variable nodes, providing a simple indicator of spectral fragility concentration.

Benchmark Harness Integration

The deterministic DPS evaluation harness now supports spectral diagnostics via:

--tanner-spectral-analysis

When enabled, the spectral analysis runs once per code instance and appends results to benchmark artifacts under:

tanner_spectral_analysis

This diagnostic remains fully optional and does not affect decoding behavior.

Determinism Guarantees

The implementation preserves strict deterministic execution:

NumPy linear algebra only

deterministic eigenvalue sorting

no randomness introduced

JSON-serializable outputs

Decoder outputs remain byte-identical when diagnostics are disabled.

Tests

New test suite:

tests/test_tanner_spectral_analysis.py

Coverage includes:

deterministic outputs across runs

correct node and edge counts

eigenvalue ordering

valid IPR ranges

node localization ordering

JSON serialization compatibility

Total tests: 33, all passing.

Architectural Compliance

Decoder core untouched

Diagnostic implemented as observational analysis layer

Fully opt-in via CLI flag

No changes to existing decoding behavior

Research Context

This release extends the v5.x attractor geometry diagnostics stack by adding structural Tanner graph analysis.

The diagnostics framework now supports deterministic measurement of:

spectral graph structure      (v5.4)
BP decision boundary distance (v5.3)
BP attractor barrier height   (v5.1)

Together these tools enable experimental investigation of how graph structure influences belief propagation dynamics and decoding failures.

title: v5.3.0 — Deterministic BP Decision Boundary Analysis
body:
Release Notes
v5.3.0 — Deterministic BP Decision Boundary Analysis

This release introduces deterministic boundary analysis for BP decoding, enabling estimation of the distance in LLR space to the nearest competing BP attractor basin.

The diagnostic probes structured perturbation directions derived from the Tanner graph and belief state, and performs deterministic binary search along each direction to determine the minimal perturbation magnitude required to change the decoder outcome.

This provides a complementary measurement to the v5.1 free-energy barrier analysis, which estimates the difficulty of escaping the current attractor basin.

Together, these diagnostics enable experimental study of BP attractor geometry, including basin stability, escape barriers, and decision boundary proximity.

Added

Deterministic BP boundary analysis diagnostic

New module:

src/qec/diagnostics/bp_boundary_analysis.py

Main API:

compute_bp_boundary_analysis(...)

Returns:

baseline_attractor
boundary_eps
boundary_direction
boundary_crossed
num_directions

where boundary_eps is the minimal perturbation magnitude that changes the decoder attractor.

Structured deterministic perturbation directions

The diagnostic probes three deterministic direction families:

Parity-check hyperplane directions
Normalized rows of the parity-check matrix.

Least-reliable bit directions
Unit vectors aligned with the lowest-magnitude LLR components.

Global sign direction
Normalized sign structure of the LLR vector.

Direction ordering is deterministic and reproducible.

Binary search boundary detection

For each direction d, the diagnostic searches along

λ' = λ + ε·d

to determine the minimal perturbation magnitude ε that causes the decoder outcome to change.

The smallest ε across all directions defines the estimated decision boundary distance.

Benchmark Harness Integration

The deterministic DPS harness now supports boundary diagnostics via:

--bp-boundary-analysis

When enabled, boundary analysis is computed per trial and appended to the benchmark artifacts under:

bp_boundary_analysis

Aggregate statistics are stored under:

bp_boundary_summary

including:

mean_boundary_eps
boundary_cross_probability
num_trials

The diagnostic remains fully optional and does not modify decoding behavior.

Determinism Guarantees

The implementation preserves strict deterministic execution:

no randomness introduced

deterministic perturbation direction ordering

binary search with fixed tolerance

LLR inputs protected via np.copy()

JSON-serializable diagnostic outputs

Decoder outputs remain byte-identical when diagnostics are disabled.

Tests

New test suite:

tests/test_bp_boundary_analysis.py

Coverage includes:

deterministic outputs across runs

successful boundary detection

no-crossing scenarios

edge cases (zero LLR, empty parity matrix)

JSON serialization compatibility

All existing tests pass without modification.

Architectural Compliance

Decoder core untouched

Diagnostic implemented as observational analysis layer

Fully opt-in via CLI flag

No changes to existing decoding behavior

Research Context

This diagnostic enables empirical comparison of:

barrier height  (v5.1)
boundary distance (v5.3)

for BP attractors.

Preliminary theory suggests that incorrect pseudocodeword attractors may exhibit both lower escape barriers and smaller boundary distances, which may explain persistent decoding failures in certain Tanner graph structures.

title: v5.2.0 — Decoder Experiment Framework and Deterministic Paired Experiments
body:
Release Notes
Overview

This release introduces the Decoder Experiment Framework, enabling controlled A/B experimentation between a frozen reference BP decoder and an experimental sandbox decoder.

The goal is to allow safe exploration of decoder modifications while preserving a permanent deterministic baseline for reproducible experiments.

All existing decoding behavior remains unchanged.

Highlights
Frozen Reference Decoder

A permanent baseline decoder has been introduced:

src/qec/decoder/bp_decoder_reference.py

This module re-exports the original bp_decode implementation and serves as the immutable reference implementation.

The reference decoder must never be modified, ensuring that historical experiments remain reproducible.

Experimental Decoder Sandbox

A new experimental decoder module has been added:

src/qec/decoder/bp_decoder_experimental.py

This module is initially identical to the reference decoder and provides a safe location for testing future decoding improvements without affecting the baseline.

Decoder Interface Layer

A lightweight decoder registry enables dynamic decoder selection:

src/qec/decoder/decoder_interface.py
DECODER_REGISTRY = {
    "reference": ...
    "experimental": ...
}

The benchmark harness can now select decoder implementations without changing the decoding API.

Decoder Selection

New CLI flag:

--decoder {reference, experimental}

Default behavior remains:

--decoder reference

This guarantees that all existing experiments produce byte-identical results.

Decoder Comparison Mode

New CLI flag:

--compare-decoders

When enabled, both decoders are executed on the same trial inputs, allowing deterministic A/B comparison experiments.

Comparison results are recorded in:

decoder_comparison
Deterministic Paired Experiments

Two optional flags ensure strict pairing of error realizations across decoders.

--paired-seed

Ensures both decoders share the same deterministic seed sequence during FER sweeps.

--paired-errors

Copies the LLR vector before each decode to prevent in-place mutation from affecting paired trials.

This guarantees both decoders observe identical error realizations.

Decoder Comparison Report

New CLI flag:

--decoder-report

Prints a comparison table summarizing decoder performance during FER experiments.

Example:

Decoder Comparison Report
-----------------------------------------------------------
mode                     distance         p   FER_ref   FER_exp      ΔFER
-----------------------------------------------------------
baseline                       32    0.0300    0.41      0.29     -0.12
rpc_only                       32    0.0300    0.53      0.34     -0.19

This makes decoder performance differences immediately visible.

Determinism Guarantees

This release preserves all core project invariants:

Baseline experiments remain byte-identical

No randomness introduced

Decoder implementation unchanged

Experiment schema unchanged

Changes confined to harness and decoder interface

Files Added
src/qec/decoder/bp_decoder_reference.py
src/qec/decoder/bp_decoder_experimental.py
src/qec/decoder/decoder_interface.py
Files Modified
src/qec/decoder/__init__.py
bench/dps_v381_eval.py
Result

QEC now supports deterministic decoder experimentation, enabling rigorous comparison of decoding algorithms while preserving a permanent baseline.

This infrastructure lays the groundwork for upcoming research on:

pseudocodeword boundary estimation

BP schedule interventions

topology-aware decoding strategies

title: v5.1.0 — Deterministic Free-Energy Barrier Estimation
body:
v5.1.0 — Deterministic Free-Energy Barrier Estimation

This release extends the BP diagnostics framework with deterministic escape-barrier analysis for belief propagation attractors.

While v5.0 introduced attractor landscape mapping, v5.1 enables direct measurement of free-energy barriers separating BP attractor basins using deterministic perturbations of the initial belief state.

The result is a quantitative tool for studying attractor stability, basin geometry, and pseudocodeword boundaries in QLDPC decoding.

New Diagnostic
Free-Energy Barrier Analysis

New module:

src/qec/diagnostics/bp_barrier_analysis.py

Core API:

compute_bp_barrier_analysis(decode_fn, llr_init, *, eps_values=None)

The algorithm performs deterministic perturbations of the initial LLR vector and observes when the BP decoder escapes its original attractor basin.

Perturbations use:

eps_values = [1e-4, 5e-4, 1e-3, 2e-3, 5e-3]
patterns   = [+1, −1, +2, −2]

For each perturbation the decoder is executed and the resulting attractor is classified using the existing fixed-point analysis framework.

The minimum perturbation magnitude that changes attractor classification is reported as the barrier height.

Returned diagnostics:

baseline_attractor
barrier_eps
escaped
num_trials

All outputs are JSON-serializable and fully deterministic.

Bench Harness Integration

The DPS benchmark harness now supports:

--bp-barrier-analysis

Barrier analysis depends on the attractor diagnostics stack and implicitly enables:

--bp-landscape-map
--bp-basin-analysis
--bp-fixed-point-analysis

Per-trial diagnostics are stored under:

bp_barrier_analysis

Aggregate statistics are reported under:

bp_barrier_summary

Metrics include:

mean_barrier_eps
escape_probability
num_trials
Determinism Guarantees

The v5.1 diagnostics layer preserves the deterministic execution guarantees of the QEC framework:

deterministic perturbation schedules

fixed perturbation patterns

no randomness

no Python hash() usage

sorted JSON output keys

byte-identical results across repeated runs

Baseline decoding outputs remain unchanged when diagnostics are disabled.

Why This Matters

Barrier estimation allows direct measurement of:

BP attractor stability

basin escape thresholds

approximate pseudocodeword boundaries

Together with the existing diagnostics stack, the QEC toolkit now provides a deterministic framework for studying BP attractor geometry and metastability in QLDPC decoding.

title: v5.0.0 — BP Attractor Landscape Mapping
body:
Summary

v5.0.0 introduces deterministic attractor landscape mapping for belief propagation (BP) decoding in QLDPC codes.
The new diagnostics module samples structured perturbations of the initial belief state and records the attractors reached by the decoder, enabling measurement of basin distributions and attractor stability.

This release builds on the diagnostic stack introduced in v4.4–v4.9 and allows the toolkit to generate a deterministic approximation of the BP attractor landscape.

All diagnostics remain trace-only and opt-in, preserving the deterministic behavior of the decoder core.

Release:
https://github.com/QSOLKCB/QEC/releases/tag/v5.0.0

Highlights
Attractor Landscape Mapping

New diagnostic module:

src/qec/diagnostics/bp_landscape_mapping.py

Primary API:

compute_bp_landscape_map(...)

The algorithm samples deterministic perturbations of the initial LLR vector and groups the resulting decoder outcomes into attractors using a CRC32 hash of the final LLR sign pattern.

This allows the toolkit to identify:

number of attractors

basin size distribution

largest basin fraction

correct vs incorrect attractor frequency

Deterministic Perturbation Sampling

Landscape exploration uses a fixed perturbation schedule:

eps_values = [1e-4, 5e-4, 1e-3, 2e-3, 5e-3]
patterns = [+1, -1, +2, -2]

These perturbations are applied to the initial LLR vector to probe nearby regions of the decoder state space.

All perturbations are deterministic, symmetric, and ordered, ensuring reproducible results.

Automatic Pseudocodeword Detection

The module identifies pseudocodeword-like attractors by detecting incorrect fixed points that remain stable under additional small perturbations.

These attractors correspond to decoding states that behave like classical BP pseudocodewords.

Reported metrics include:

num_pseudocodewords
pseudocodeword_fraction
pseudocodeword_ids
Benchmark Harness Integration

The DPS benchmark harness now supports:

--bp-landscape-map

When enabled, the harness performs attractor landscape sampling and records results under:

bp_landscape_map

Diagnostics remain fully optional and do not modify decoder behavior.

Tests

New test suite:

tests/test_bp_landscape_mapping.py

Coverage includes:

single-attractor cases

multi-attractor landscapes

pseudocodeword detection

deterministic behavior

JSON serialization

All tests pass with deterministic outputs.

Architectural Guarantees

v5.0.0 preserves all core framework invariants:

decoder core unchanged (src/qec/decoder/)

construction layer unchanged (src/qec/construction/)

deterministic execution preserved

schema unchanged

baseline decoding outputs remain byte-identical when diagnostics are disabled

Diagnostics Stack

The QEC toolkit now provides a layered framework for analyzing BP behavior:

Version	Capability
v4.4	BP regime dynamics
v4.5	regime transition tracing
v4.6	phase diagram aggregation
v4.7	freeze detection
v4.8	fixed-point trap classification
v4.9	basin-of-attraction analysis
v5.0	attractor landscape mapping

Together these tools enable systematic study of BP attractor geometry and decoding stability in QLDPC Tanner graphs.

Why This Matters

These diagnostics allow direct measurement of:

incorrect BP attractor probability

attractor basin size

approximate pseudocodeword structures

With v5.0.0, the QSOLKCB/QEC toolkit evolves from a decoder implementation into a deterministic experimental framework for studying BP attractor landscapes in QLDPC decoding.

title: v4.9.0 — Basin-of-Attraction and Boundary Analysis
body:
Summary

v4.9.0 introduces deterministic diagnostics for basin-of-attraction analysis in belief propagation (BP).
The new module estimates how frequently BP converges to each attractor and identifies the minimum perturbation magnitude that changes the attractor outcome, providing a practical estimate of BP basin boundaries.

This release extends the diagnostics stack introduced in v4.4–v4.8 and enables direct measurement of BP attractor geometry in QLDPC decoding.

All diagnostics remain trace-only and opt-in, preserving deterministic decoder behavior.

Release:
https://github.com/QSOLKCB/QEC/releases/tag/v4.9.0

Highlights
Basin-of-Attraction Diagnostics

New module:

src/qec/diagnostics/bp_basin_analysis.py

Primary API:

compute_bp_basin_analysis(...)

The analysis applies deterministic perturbations to the initial LLR vector and classifies the resulting decoder outcomes.

Perturbation schedule:

eps_values = [1e-4, 5e-4, 1e-3, 2e-3, 5e-3]
patterns = [+1, -1, +2, -2]

Each perturbed decode is classified using the v4.8 fixed-point trap analysis.

Basin Statistics

The module estimates the probability that BP converges to each attractor type:

basin_correct_probability
basin_incorrect_probability

Counts are also reported:

num_perturbations
num_correct
num_incorrect
num_degenerate

These metrics approximate the relative size of attractor basins under deterministic perturbations.

Basin Boundary Estimation

v4.9.0 introduces a lightweight estimator for the distance to the nearest basin boundary.

The algorithm identifies the smallest perturbation magnitude that changes the attractor classification.

Output field:

basin_boundary_eps

This provides a practical estimate of the pseudocodeword basin boundary.

title: v4.8.0 — Deterministic Fixed-Point Trap Analysis

The new module classifies BP outcomes into distinct fixed-point categories, enabling systematic measurement of incorrect attractor convergence in QLDPC decoding.

This release builds on the diagnostics stack introduced in v4.4–v4.7 and provides the instrumentation needed to analyze fixed-point trapping as a failure mechanism in BP decoders.

title: v4.7.1 — BP Phase Transition Experiments & Analysis Toolkit
body:
v4.7.1 — BP Phase Transition Experiments & Analysis Toolkit
Summary

This patch release adds deterministic experiment tooling and analysis scripts used to investigate BP dynamical regimes in the QSOLKCB/QEC decoder.

Large-scale deterministic experiments were performed to test whether decoding failures arise from metastability or freeze dynamics. Results show that under the BSC syndrome channel, BP converges immediately to fixed points and does not exhibit oscillatory or freeze behavior.

This release packages the deterministic experiment harness and analysis artifacts used for that investigation.

Highlights
BP Phase Transition Experiment Script

Added deterministic experiment runner:

bench/bp_phase_transition_experiments.py

This script evaluates BP dynamics across a grid of:

distance ∈ {5,7,9,11,13}
noise ∈ {0.001 … 0.015}

Metrics collected:

FER
freeze_probability
metastable_probability
freeze_score
switch_rate
event_rate
mean_iterations
Extended Noise Sweep Analysis

Additional sweep performed across:

p ∈ {0.01, 0.02, 0.03, 0.05, 0.08}

confirming:

freeze_probability = 0
switch_rate = 0
event_rate = 0

across all evaluated parameter points.

This indicates decoding failures arise from incorrect fixed-point convergence, not metastable trapping.

Deterministic Analysis Artifacts

Generated artifacts include:

bp_phase_transition_data.json
bp_phase_transition_data_extended.json
bp_phase_transition_plot.png
bp_phase_transition_plot_extended.png

and a full report:

BP_DYNAMICS_EXPERIMENTS_v4.7.1.md

All artifacts are deterministic and reproducible.

Key Observations

Experiments revealed:

BP reaches a fixed point within ~1 iteration
energy traces remain flat
LLR sign changes = 0

As noise increases, FER rises smoothly but no dynamical phase transition is observed.

The DPS inversion observed in earlier benchmarks therefore arises from incorrect attractor selection, not oscillatory BP dynamics.

Determinism

All experiment runs were verified deterministic:

identical JSON outputs
identical plots
identical SHA-256 hashes

Diagnostics remain observation-only and introduce no changes to decoder behavior.

Architecture

This release preserves all architectural guarantees:

decoder core unchanged

construction layer unchanged

deterministic execution preserved

diagnostics remain opt-in

title: v4.7.0 — Deterministic BP Freeze Detection
body:
Release Notes
v4.7.0 — Deterministic BP Freeze Detection

This release introduces deterministic early freeze detection for belief propagation (BP) decoding. The new diagnostics module detects when decoding dynamics enter a metastable regime where the decoder is effectively trapped despite continued iterations.

Freeze detection builds on the BP dynamics diagnostics stack introduced in earlier releases, enabling systematic analysis of metastability and trapping behavior in QLDPC Tanner graphs.

Highlights
BP Freeze Detection

New diagnostics module:

src/qec/diagnostics/bp_freeze_detection.py

provides:

compute_bp_freeze_detection(...)

This function analyzes BP iteration traces and computes a deterministic freeze score derived from BP dynamics metrics, allowing detection of metastable decoding behavior before iteration limits are reached.

Returned diagnostics include:

freeze_detected
freeze_iteration
freeze_score
freeze_regime

All outputs are JSON-serializable and deterministic.

Benchmark Harness Integration

The DPS benchmark harness now supports the new CLI flag:

--bp-freeze-detection

Example usage:

python bench/dps_v381_eval.py \
  --bp-dynamics \
  --bp-transitions \
  --bp-phase-diagram \
  --bp-freeze-detection

This adds a new artifact field:

bp_freeze_detection

allowing freeze behavior to be analyzed alongside regime transitions and phase diagrams.

Diagnostics Stack

The QEC diagnostics framework now includes:

Version	Capability
v4.4	BP regime classification
v4.5	regime transition analysis
v4.6	phase diagram aggregation
v4.7	early metastability / freeze detection

Together these diagnostics enable deterministic study of BP dynamical phases, trapping behavior, and metastability in QLDPC decoding.

Robustness Improvements

This release also strengthens input validation:

strict validation that llr_trace and energy_trace lengths match

explicit error handling for malformed diagnostics traces

improved test coverage for validation and threshold behavior

These safeguards prevent silent trace misalignment and ensure deterministic diagnostics behavior.

Test Results

7 skipped

New tests cover:

freeze detection behavior

threshold suppression behavior

trace validation edge cases

CI status: green

Architectural Guarantees

v4.7.0 preserves all QEC architectural invariants:

Decoder core unchanged (src/qec/decoder/)

Construction layer unchanged (src/qec/construction/)

Diagnostics strictly opt-in

Deterministic artifacts preserved
