Research Context

Freeze detection provides instrumentation for studying metastable trapping and dynamical freezing in belief propagation decoding, supporting investigation of phenomena such as trapping sets, oscillatory convergence, and potential spin-glass–like dynamics in large Tanner graphs.

title: v4.5.0 — Deterministic BP Regime Transition Analysis
body:
Release Notes
v4.5.0 — Deterministic BP Regime Transition Analysis

This release extends the diagnostics framework with deterministic regime transition analysis for belief propagation (BP) decoding. The new functionality enables temporal analysis of decoder dynamics, including regime switching, dwell-time measurement, and detection of large transition events.

The diagnostics remain strictly observational and opt-in, preserving deterministic decoding behavior and byte-identical baseline outputs.

Highlights
Sliding-Window Regime Classification

Applies the v4.4 BP regime classifier across sliding iteration windows to produce a per-iteration regime trace describing decoder dynamical behavior over time.

Regime Transition Detection

Automatically detects regime changes between iterations and records transitions in the form:

{t, from, to, event}

allowing detailed analysis of decoder state evolution.

Dwell-Time Analysis

Measures contiguous durations spent in each regime, enabling identification of long metastable states or oscillatory periods.

Instanton-Style Event Detection

Flags large energy transitions that exceed a configurable threshold relative to the median energy change, capturing rare barrier-crossing events during decoding.

Transition Matrix and Summary Metrics

Provides deterministic summary statistics including:

switch_rate

max_dwell

freeze_score

num_events

These metrics help characterize metastability and regime switching behavior.

New Diagnostics Module
src/qec/diagnostics/bp_regime_trace.py

which produces:

transitions

Benchmark Harness Integration

--bp-transitions

Example:

python bench/dps_v381_eval.py --bp-dynamics --bp-transitions

This records regime traces and transition summaries alongside existing DPS evaluation outputs.

Stability Improvements

Added strict validation ensuring llr_trace and energy_trace lengths match

Prevented regime-trace parameters from leaking into the v4.4 metrics API

Added edge-case tests for mismatched trace lengths

title: v4.4.0 — Deterministic BP Dynamics Regime Analysis
body:
Release Notes
Overview

v4.4.0 introduces Deterministic BP Dynamics Regime Analysis, expanding the QEC diagnostics framework with a trace-based metric suite and classifier for analyzing global belief propagation (BP) behavior on QLDPC Tanner graphs.

This release completes the first full BP observability stack within the toolkit, enabling systematic analysis of decoder dynamics such as oscillation, metastability, trapping-set behavior, and chaotic inference regimes.

All diagnostics remain strictly observational and opt-in, preserving the deterministic decoding guarantees of the QEC framework.

Baseline decoding outputs remain byte-identical when diagnostics are disabled.

BP Dynamics Metrics

New deterministic metrics derived from BP iteration traces:

Metric	Description
MSI	Metastability Index — detects plateau behavior with residual oscillations
CPI	Cycle Periodicity Index — deterministic detection of repeating BP states
TSL	Trapping Set Likelihood — persistent sign disagreement indicator
LEC	Local Energy Curvature — second-difference structure of the energy trace
CVNE	Correction-Vector Norm Entropy (optional when correction vectors available)
GOS	Global Oscillation Score — aggregate oscillation signal
EDS	Energy Descent Smoothness — monotonicity of energy descent
BTI	Basin Transition Indicator — energy barrier crossings and state transitions

These metrics provide a deterministic view of the energy landscape and dynamical behavior of BP decoding.

Deterministic Regime Classifier

A deterministic rule-based classifier maps BP behavior into six regimes:

stable_convergence

oscillatory_convergence

metastable_state

trapping_set_regime

correction_cycling

chaotic_behavior

Each classification includes explicit evidence and threshold comparisons, ensuring interpretability and reproducibility.

Bench Harness Integration

The DPS benchmark harness now supports an opt-in diagnostics flag:

--bp-dynamics

When enabled:

BP iteration traces are analyzed using the new metric suite

Results are appended to benchmark artifacts under:

bp_dynamics

No existing artifact schema or baseline decoding path is modified.

Determinism Guarantees

The v4.4 diagnostics layer preserves the determinism guarantees of the QEC framework:

No randomness introduced

No Python hash() usage

Deterministic CRC32 state signatures

JSON-serializable artifact output

Byte-identical outputs verified across repeated runs

Sequential determinism validation confirmed identical SHA-256 artifacts across multiple benchmark executions.

Validation Improvements

This release also introduces stronger input validation for diagnostic traces:

Explicit trace-length validation between llr_trace, energy_trace, and optional correction_vectors

Per-iteration LLR vector length consistency checks

_normalize_llr_vector now rejects tensors with rank > 2 to prevent silent truncation

These safeguards ensure that malformed traces fail fast rather than producing invalid metrics.

Test Results
59 passed, 0 failed

Full repository CI remains green.

Architectural Compliance

This release preserves all core system invariants:

Decoder core unchanged (src/qec/decoder/)

Code construction layer unchanged (src/qec/construction/)

Schema unchanged

Diagnostics strictly observational

Baseline decoding behavior identical when diagnostics are disabled

Research Context

v4.4.0 completes the first layered diagnostics stack for studying BP decoding dynamics:

Version	Capability
v4.1	Basin-switch detection
v4.2	Energy landscape diagnostics
v4.3	Iteration trajectory diagnostics
v4.4	BP regime classification

These tools enable systematic investigation of distance performance scaling (DPS) inversion observed under syndrome-only inference.

Author

Trent Slade
QSOL-IMC

title: v4.3.0 — Deterministic Iteration-Trace Diagnostics for BP Decoder Dynamics
body:
Release Notes
Overview

v4.3.0 introduces a deterministic diagnostics layer for analyzing the iteration dynamics of belief propagation (BP) decoders in LDPC and QLDPC simulations.

The new diagnostics operate purely on iteration traces (LLR trajectories, energy traces, and correction vectors) and provide insight into decoder behaviors such as trapping sets, oscillatory message passing, and unstable convergence.

These metrics are computed post-decode, ensuring that the BP decoder itself remains unchanged.

New Diagnostics
Persistent Error Indicator (PEI)

Detects potential trapping sets by identifying variable nodes that remain in error across consecutive iterations.

Belief Oscillation Index (BOI)

Counts LLR sign changes to detect oscillatory belief propagation.

Zero LLR values are treated as non-negative, ensuring consistent sign-flip detection.

Oscillation Depth (OD)

Measures the amplitude of LLR oscillations over recent iterations, providing insight into instability magnitude.

Convergence Instability Score (CIS)

Computes the variance of the energy trace during the final iterations to detect unstable convergence behavior.

Correction Vector Fluctuation (CVF)

Measures the magnitude of changes between successive correction vectors to detect correction cycling.

When correction vectors are unavailable, CVF fields are returned as None to avoid misleading interpretations.

Benchmark Harness Integration

The benchmarking harness now supports optional iteration diagnostics.

When enabled, iteration-trace metrics are computed alongside existing energy-landscape diagnostics.

These results allow the framework to analyze both:

energy landscape structure

decoder trajectory through that landscape

Correctness Improvements

During review, two diagnostic correctness issues were addressed before release:

BOI sign logic corrected to treat zero LLR values as non-negative using deterministic sign mapping.

CVF metric behavior clarified so correction-vector metrics are disabled when correction vectors are not available.

Additional edge-case tests were added to validate both behaviors.

Architectural Guarantees

The following invariants remain unchanged:

Decoder core unchanged

Construction modules untouched

No schema changes

Diagnostics operate as a separate analysis layer

Decoder outputs remain byte-identical when diagnostics are disabled

Test Results
1042 passed
7 skipped
0 failures

New tests include:

BOI zero-handling edge cases

composite metric validation when CVF is unavailable

deterministic diagnostics validation

Research Context

This release extends the deterministic diagnostic framework introduced in v4.1–v4.2.

The system now supports three complementary diagnostic layers:

v4.1 — Basin-switch classification

v4.2 — Free-energy landscape metrics

v4.3 — Iteration-trace dynamics diagnostics

Together these tools enable systematic analysis of BP decoder behavior in both classical LDPC and quantum LDPC error-correction systems.

title: v4.2.1 — Diagnostics Refactor and Test Hardening
body:
Release Notes
Overview

v4.2.1 is a patch release that improves the diagnostics layer introduced in v4.2.0.
This release eliminates redundant BP decodes in landscape diagnostics, adds configurability to the escape-energy sweep, and strengthens test coverage.

All changes are internal improvements. Decoder behavior and deterministic outputs remain unchanged.

Improvements
Shared Perturbation Decode Results

Landscape diagnostics previously executed redundant perturbation decodes when computing basin classification and landscape metrics.

This release introduces an internal helper:

_run_perturbation_decodes()

which executes the baseline and ±ε decodes once and shares the results between:

classify_basin_switch()
compute_landscape_metrics()

Result:

Reduced redundant BP decodes

Improved diagnostic runtime

No change to diagnostic outputs

Configurable Escape Energy Sweep

compute_escape_energy() now accepts an optional parameter:

eps_values

Example:

compute_escape_energy(..., eps_values=[0.01, 0.02])

If not provided, the default sweep remains unchanged:

[1e-3, 2e-3, 5e-3, 1e-2]

This allows experiments to trade off resolution vs runtime without modifying library code.

Strengthened Landscape Integration Tests

Integration tests were hardened to ensure landscape metrics are always exercised.

Changes include:

explicit assertions verifying multi-iteration energy traces

guaranteed validation of landscape metric fields

improved fixture parameters to prevent single-iteration decodes from bypassing checks

Added Test Coverage for eps_values

New tests verify:

custom epsilon schedules are accepted and respected

empty epsilon schedules (eps_values=[]) safely return None escape energies without raising exceptions

These tests protect the configurable sweep logic from regressions.

Architecture & Determinism

This release preserves all architectural guarantees:

decoder core unchanged

BP scheduling unchanged

schema unchanged

deterministic execution preserved

baseline decoding outputs remain byte-identical

Diagnostics remain fully contained within the diagnostics and benchmarking layers.

Test Results

title: v4.2.0 — Deterministic Energy Landscape Metrics
body:
Release Notes
Overview

This release introduces deterministic energy-landscape diagnostics for belief-propagation (BP) decoding in QLDPC simulations. Building on the basin-switch classification introduced in v4.1.0, v4.2.0 adds quantitative metrics that characterize the stability and structure of the decoder’s attractor landscape under small deterministic perturbations.

These diagnostics allow experiments to measure how stable a decoding solution is, how far nearby attractors are, and how much perturbation is required to escape the current basin.

All diagnostics operate without modifying the decoder algorithm and preserve the repository’s architectural guarantees of determinism and reproducibility.

New Landscape Metrics
Basin Stability Index (BSI)

Measures how often small deterministic perturbations return the decoder to the same solution basin.

BSI = (# perturbations yielding same correction as baseline) / (# perturbations)

Values range from 0.0 to 1.0, where:

1.0 → fully stable basin

0.0 → unstable basin

Attractor Distance (AD)

Measures the Hamming distance between decoding corrections produced by perturbations and the baseline decode.

Reported values:

attractor_distance_max
attractor_distance_mean

These quantify how far neighboring attractors lie in correction space.

Escape Energy (EE)

Estimates the minimum perturbation magnitude required to leave the current attractor basin.

A deterministic epsilon sweep is performed:

[1e-3, 2e-3, 5e-3, 1e-2]

Directional escape barriers are reported:

escape_energy_plus
escape_energy_minus
escape_energy

The final escape energy is the minimum of the directional barriers.

This approach is inspired by spin-glass energy-landscape analysis and provides a deterministic approximation of basin barrier height.

Composite Landscape Metrics

A new composite helper:

compute_landscape_metrics()

returns:

basin_switch_class
basin_stability_index
attractor_distance_max
attractor_distance_mean
escape_energy
escape_energy_plus
escape_energy_minus

This integrates the v4.1.0 basin classifier with the new v4.2.0 landscape metrics.

Harness Integration

The DPS evaluation harness now computes landscape metrics automatically when landscape mode is enabled.

enable_landscape=True

This allows full landscape diagnostics to be collected during benchmarking runs.

Determinism & Architecture Guarantees

This release preserves all architectural invariants defined in CLAUDE.md:

Decoder core unchanged

BP message passing untouched

Scheduling semantics unchanged

Schema unchanged

Deterministic execution preserved

Baseline decoding outputs remain byte-identical

Landscape diagnostics operate entirely in the diagnostics and benchmarking layers.

Testing

New tests verify:

title: v4.1.0 — Deterministic Basin Switch Diagnostics
body:
Release Notes
Overview

This release introduces an improved deterministic basin-switch detection system for analyzing the energy landscape of belief propagation (BP) decoding.

The new diagnostics distinguish between metastable oscillation, shallow perturbation sensitivity, and true basin switching, enabling deeper insight into decoder behavior without modifying the decoder core.

The implementation remains strictly diagnostic-only, preserving the architectural invariants of the QEC toolkit:

decoder semantics unchanged

deterministic execution preserved

schema stability maintained

baseline outputs byte-identical

This release strengthens the energy-landscape tooling used for decoder stability analysis and benchmarking.

Highlights
Improved Basin Switch Detection

The deterministic perturbation probe has been expanded to classify decoder behavior into four regimes:

none — baseline and perturbations converge to the same attractor

shallow_sensitivity — perturbations alter trajectory but converge to the same solution

metastable_oscillation — oscillatory or unstable energy trace behavior

true_basin_switch — perturbations move decoding into a different attractor basin

The classifier uses diagnostic signals including:

energy trace stability

gradient sign flips

convergence behavior

final correction differences

deterministic perturbation probes

This enables reliable identification of energy landscape structure without modifying BP internals.

Deterministic Perturbation Probing

Basin classification uses symmetric deterministic probes:

baseline = decode(llr)
plus     = decode(llr + ε·sign(llr))
minus    = decode(llr − ε·sign(llr))

where:

ε = _BASIN_EPSILON

The perturbation direction uses a canonical deterministic sign function.

All diagnostics remain fully reproducible under identical inputs.

Diagnostics Infrastructure Improvements
Canonical Perturbation Sign

Introduced _deterministic_sign() to ensure consistent perturbation direction:

zero values deterministically map to +1

input arrays remain immutable

a fresh array is always allocated

This eliminates potential drift in perturbation handling.

Trace Convergence Helper

_check_convergence has been renamed to:

_trace_converged()

to clarify that it evaluates energy trace stability, not decoder convergence.

Shared Tolerance Constants

Energy comparison thresholds now share a single constant:

_TRACE_TOLERANCE = 1e-6

This value is used consistently by:

_trace_converged()

_ENERGY_DELTA_THRESHOLD

ensuring threshold consistency across diagnostics.

Test Improvements

Testing infrastructure has been strengthened:

deterministic sign helper tests added

helper test suite renamed for clarity (TestClassifierHelpers)

aggregation validation added to ensure basin_class_counts matches classification results

Full test suite status:

973 passed
7 skipped
0 failed
Architectural Guarantees

This release preserves all core architectural invariants defined in CLAUDE.md:

decoder core untouched

belief propagation scheduling unchanged

schema unchanged

determinism preserved

artifact identity stability maintained

Decoder outputs under identical inputs remain byte-identical across all minor releases.

Repository Consistency Updates

README updated to reflect v4.1.0 as the current release

shared constants introduced to prevent drift

documentation synchronized with implementation

Upgrade Notes

No user-facing API changes.

Existing simulations, benchmarks, and decoder workflows remain fully compatible.

The new diagnostics are automatically available through the energy landscape analysis tooling.

Next Steps

Future roadmap work will continue expanding deterministic diagnostics for analyzing decoder energy landscapes and stability behavior.

title: v4.0.0 — Free-Energy Landscape Diagnostics for BP Decoding
body:
v4.0.0 — BP Free-Energy Landscape Diagnostics

This release introduces a diagnostics layer for analyzing belief-propagation (BP) energy dynamics during QLDPC decoding.

The new tooling enables the DPS benchmark harness to analyze decoder convergence regimes, including plateau behavior, barrier crossings, and geometry-induced basin switching.

Decoder core behavior remains unchanged. All diagnostics operate purely on energy traces and decoder outputs, preserving deterministic execution.

New Features
Free-Energy Landscape Diagnostics

New module:

src/qec/diagnostics/energy_landscape.py

Provides deterministic analysis utilities for BP energy traces:

compute_energy_gradient

compute_energy_curvature

detect_plateau

detect_local_minima

detect_barrier_crossings

classify_energy_landscape

detect_basin_switch

These functions allow systematic characterization of BP convergence behavior beyond FER metrics.

Basin-Switch Detection

Introduces a deterministic perturbation experiment to detect geometry-induced basin switching.

A small perturbation is applied to the LLR vector:

llr_perturbed = llr + ε * sign(llr)
ε = 1e-3

If the decoder converges to a different correction or final energy, the trial is classified as a basin switch.

The detector is numerically stable and deterministic, handling sign(0) safely.

DPS Harness Integration

The DPS evaluation harness now supports landscape diagnostics via:

--landscape

When enabled the harness records:

energy traces

landscape classification

basin switching statistics

Example run:

PYTHONPATH=. python bench/dps_v381_eval.py \
  --landscape \
  --trials 200 \
  --distances 5 7 \
  --p-values 0.03
Experimental Observations

Validation runs revealed geometry-induced basin switching in RPC-augmented centered-field decoding modes.

Example statistics:

Mode	d=5	d=7
rpc_centered	~15%	~6%
rpc_centered_prior	~13%	~4%
other modes	0%	0%

This suggests the interaction between RPC augmentation and centered syndrome-field geometry can introduce multiple BP convergence basins under moderate noise.

Performance Improvements

The basin-switch detector now reuses the existing decode result from the harness instead of running a redundant baseline decode.

This reduces diagnostic overhead significantly when landscape analysis is enabled.

Stability Fixes

Fixed potential double-application of geometry post-processing in basin-switch experiments.

Improved perturbation stability by handling sign(0) deterministically.

Ensured symmetric comparison between baseline and perturbed decodes.

Tests

New diagnostics test suite:

tests/test_energy_landscape.py

Coverage includes:

gradient computation

curvature computation

plateau detection

barrier detection

basin switching

Full test results:

945 passed
7 skipped

No regressions detected.

Determinism

All diagnostics remain fully deterministic.

When landscape analysis is disabled, decoding behavior is bit-identical to previous versions.

Architecture

The diagnostics layer is strictly observational:

BP decoder
    ↓
energy trace
    ↓
diagnostics layer
    ↓
DPS benchmark reporting

No modifications were made to BP message updates, scheduling, or correction logic.

Next Steps

Future releases will extend diagnostics with:

metastable oscillation detection

enhanced basin classification

deeper BP dynamics analysis tools.

These allow controlled scaling and normalization of geometry-derived fields while preserving deterministic decoder behavior.

Geometry post-processing is applied after field construction and pseudo-prior injection but before belief propagation decoding.

Geometry Processing Pipeline

Geometry field processing has been centralized into a shared utility:

src/qec/channel/geometry_post.py

This module implements deterministic geometry normalization and scaling, including safeguards against near-zero variance fields.

The shared pipeline is now used by:

BP adapter

DPS evaluation harness

geometry control tests

This eliminates duplicated logic and prevents normalization drift across experimental pathways.

DPS Harness Improvements

The DPS evaluation harness now supports additional geometry evaluation modes for controlled field-strength experiments.

New modes include normalized and amplified geometry fields, enabling systematic exploration of geometry influence on decoding behavior.

All evaluation remains strictly deterministic:

fixed RNG seed

pre-generated error instances

deterministic loop ordering

reproducible FER/DPS measurements

Architecture Improvements

Refactoring improves separation between:

geometry field construction
geometry post-processing
decoder execution

Decoder core logic remains unchanged.

All geometry interventions remain opt-in through StructuralConfig.

Tests

geometry strength scaling

geometry normalization behavior

deterministic execution across geometry modes

DPS harness mode definitions

the decoder executes identically to previous versions.

All baseline invariants remain preserved.

title: v3.9.0 — Channel Geometry Interventions & BP Energy Diagnostics
body:
Release Notes
Overview

v3.9.0 introduces deterministic channel-geometry interventions and belief propagation energy diagnostics to the QSOLKCB/QEC framework.

This release expands the structural experimentation platform introduced in v3.8.x and provides new tools for analyzing the information geometry of syndrome-only inference in QLDPC decoding.

Baseline decoder behavior remains byte-identical when structural features are disabled.

New Features
Channel Geometry Interventions

Two deterministic interventions were added to the channel model layer.

Centered Field Projection

Removes the uniform syndrome bias before projection:

b = 1 − 2s
b_centered = b − mean(b)

LLR = Hᵀ b_centered

Purpose:

eliminate global field collapse

restore directional likelihood structure

preserve deterministic decoding

Pseudo-Prior Injection

Adds a weak deterministic prior derived from parity structure:

parity_bias = Hᵀ(1 − 2s)

LLR ← LLR + κ · parity_bias

Default:

κ = 0.25

Properties:

deterministic

no oracle leakage

opt-in structural feature

BP Energy Diagnostics

A new optional diagnostic records per-iteration BP energy traces:

E = − Σ (LLR_i · belief_i)

This allows analysis of:

convergence dynamics

oscillatory BP behavior

likelihood alignment

Energy tracing is purely diagnostic and does not alter decoder behavior.

DPS Harness Expansion

The deterministic evaluation harness now supports geometry-intervention modes:

Mode	Centered	Prior	geom_v1	RPC
baseline	F	F	F	F
centered	T	F	F	F
prior	F	T	F	F
centered_prior	T	T	F	F
geom_centered	T	F	T	F
geom_centered_prior	T	T	T	F
rpc_centered	T	F	F	T
rpc_centered_prior	T	T	F	T

All modes reuse identical deterministic error instances.

Stability Improvements

Stabilized bp_decode return structure across optional features

Added epsilon threshold for DPS sign detection

Strengthened baseline invariance tests

Verification

Full verification completed prior to release.

904 passed
0 failed
7 skipped

Baseline decoder outputs remain identical when structural features are disabled.

Experimental Observations

Initial geometry-intervention experiments show:

oracle-LLR modes converge in one iteration

geometry-derived LLR modes produce meaningful BP dynamics

RPC augmentation introduces oscillatory energy descent

centered field and pseudo-prior interventions are currently collinear

No DPS sign flip was observed within the tested parameter regime.

These results support the hypothesis that the DPS inversion arises from constraint-level information geometry rather than message-scaling effects.

Architecture Status

Decoder invariants remain intact:

BP flooding schedule unchanged

BP layered schedule unchanged

_bp_postprocess() unchanged

baseline decoder outputs preserved

All new functionality is opt-in.

Next Steps
v3.9.1

Focused exploration of DPS-flipping deterministic interventions, including:

constraint-weighted projections

structured pseudo-priors

parity amplification

v4.0.0

Planned extension of energy diagnostics toward BP free-energy landscape analysis.

Small is beautiful.
Determinism is holy.
Negative results are data.

title: v3.7.0 — Deterministic Uniformly Reweighted BP (URW) & Geometry Audit
body:
📦 Release Notes
Overview

v3.7.0 introduces Uniformly Reweighted Belief Propagation (URW-BP) as a strictly opt-in deterministic inference-geometry variant for QLDPC decoding.

This release explores whether uniform damping of check-to-variable messages can mitigate the observed Distance Preservation Slope (DPS) inversion under syndrome-only channel inference.

Baseline behavior remains unchanged.

What’s New
Uniformly Reweighted BP (URW)

New BP mode:

mode="min_sum_urw"

New parameter:

urw_rho ∈ (0, 1]

URW modifies the min-sum check-to-variable update as:

R_{j→i} ← ρ · R_{j→i}

Where:

ρ = 1.0 → bit-identical to baseline min_sum

ρ < 1.0 → uniformly damped check influence

Fully deterministic

No adaptive behavior

No stochastic components

Flooding and layered schedules both supported.

Structural Guarantees

Baseline decoders unchanged.

Golden artifact hash unchanged.

_bp_postprocess() untouched.

Scheduling semantics untouched.

Schema version unchanged.

Identity stability preserved.

Determinism verified across repeated runs.

All existing tests pass.

New URW tests added.

URW is strictly opt-in and introduces zero drift when disabled.

Geometry Probe — DPS Audit

A controlled DPS sweep was executed under:

channel_model="bsc_syndrome"

distances [8, 12, 16]

p [0.01, 0.02, 0.03, 0.04]

300 trials per point

Findings

No urw_rho value produced negative DPS.

DPS magnitude varied slightly but non-monotonically.

No stable rho window reduced inversion meaningfully.

URW does not correct syndrome-only distance scaling inversion.

Conclusion:

Uniform scalar reweighting is insufficient to alter the structural inversion regime under syndrome-only inference.

Interpretation

v3.7.0 eliminates uniform reweighting as a viable correction mechanism for DPS inversion.

This narrows the remaining intervention space to higher-tier structural modifications (e.g., constraint augmentation or objective deformation).

The inversion appears structural to the information geometry of syndrome-only decoding rather than an artifact of loop overcounting magnitude.

Files Changed

src/qec_qldpc_codes.py

tests/test_urw_bp_v370.py

CHANGELOG.md

No dependency changes.
No schema changes.
No canonicalization changes.

Design Philosophy

Small is beautiful.
Determinism is holy.
Negative results are data.

If it cannot be reproduced byte-for-byte, it is not a baseline.

title: v3.6.0 — Deterministic Posterior-Aware Combination-Sweep OSD
body:
📦 Release Notes
Overview

v3.6.0 introduces a new opt-in postprocess mode:

postprocess="mp_osd_cs"

This extends combination-sweep OSD (osd_cs) by using posterior LLR magnitude (abs(L_post)) instead of channel LLR magnitude for reliability ordering during information-set selection and sweep enumeration.

This release completes the ordering-tier escalation initiated in v3.5.0 (mp_osd1).

Default decoder behavior remains bit-identical.

What’s New
Posterior-Aware Combination-Sweep OSD (mp_osd_cs)

Runs inner BP with postprocess=None and llr_history=1

Extracts posterior beliefs L_post

Uses abs(L_post) for column reliability ordering

Reuses existing osd_cs_lam for sweep depth

Enforces never-degrade guarantee

Preserves deterministic tie-breaking

Implementation follows the same early-return wrapper pattern used by mp_osd1.

Architectural Guarantees

BP flooding and layered loops unchanged

Scheduling semantics unchanged

_bp_postprocess() untouched

No schema version change

No identity drift for baseline decoders

No new configuration parameters

No randomness introduced

Full test suite passes (baseline invariance verified)

This is a strictly additive feature.

Structural Probe Findings (Ordering Sensitivity)

A controlled lam-sensitivity probe was performed under:

channel_model="bsc_syndrome"

lam ∈ {1, 2, 3}

distances {8, 12, 16}

10,800 total trials

Findings:

osd_cs and mp_osd_cs produced byte-identical corrections across all trials

No ordering sensitivity observed at tested scales

DPS inversion persists under syndrome-only channel

Increasing lam did not change candidate selection

Interpretation:

Under uniform channel LLR (BSC-syndrome), posterior-aware ordering does not alter decoded outputs at tested code sizes. The ordering pathway is structurally verified but not expressive in this regime.

This isolates DPS inversion as an upstream inference or information-model phenomenon rather than an ordering deficiency.

Research Implication

v3.6.0 exhausts the ordering-based escalation tier.

Future work (v3.7.x) will explore inference-geometry and/or channel-model directions to address persistent distance scaling inversion.

Version Scope

This release is stable, deterministic, and production-safe.
Tag recommended.

title: v3.5.0 — Posterior-Aware OSD-1 (mp_osd1) & Syndrome-Only Inversion Mitigation
body:
📄 Release Notes
v3.5.0 — Deterministic MP-Aware OSD-1

This release introduces a new opt-in postprocess mode:

postprocess="mp_osd1"

mp_osd1 is a message-passing-aware variant of OSD-1 that orders flips by posterior LLR magnitude (abs(L_post)) rather than channel LLR magnitude.

The implementation follows the early-dispatch wrapper pattern established in v3.4.0:

Inner BP run with postprocess=None and llr_history=1

Immediate return if BP converges

Otherwise applies OSD-1 with posterior-based reliability ordering

Deterministic tie-breaking

Never-degrade guarantee enforced

No baseline behavior is altered.

Structural Result — Syndrome-Only DPS Probe

Before tagging, a controlled structural probe was executed under:

channel_model="bsc_syndrome"

distances [5, 7]

p = 0.015

150 trials

min-sum flooding schedule

None

osd1

mp_osd1

guided_decimation

osd1	+0.0673

All decoders remain in the positive-slope (inverted) regime under syndrome-only noise.

However:

It reduces inversion magnitude relative to:

Standard OSD-1

Guided decimation

FER growth between distances is measurably smaller under posterior-aware ordering.

This indicates:

Posterior LLR magnitude is a structurally superior reliability metric compared to channel LLR magnitude under syndrome-only inference.

Inversion is not eliminated, but its magnitude is reduced.

Added

mp_osd1 is the first postprocess in QEC that:

Consumes posterior belief information

Preserves deterministic guarantees

Reduces structural inversion magnitude under realistic channel conditions

It establishes posterior-aware ordering as a viable direction for future decoder interventions.

If it cannot be reproduced byte-for-byte, it is not a baseline.

title: v3.4.0 — Deterministic Guided Decimation for Syndrome-Only BP
body:
## v3.4.0 — Deterministic Guided Decimation

This release introduces a new opt-in postprocess mode:

    postprocess="guided_decimation"

It implements deterministic belief-propagation–guided variable freezing as a minimal structural intervention for syndrome-only decoding.

---

## Motivation

Recent geometry-aware diagnostics showed:

- 100% positive DPS slopes under syndrome-only decoding
- BSI ≈ 0 (iteration increases ineffective)
- Small but non-zero SSI

These results indicate a structural trapping/degeneracy issue rather than an iteration-budget problem.

v3.4.0 adds a bounded, deterministic structural intervention without modifying:

- BP message-passing semantics
- Scheduling logic
- `_bp_postprocess()` behavior
- Schema versions
- Default decoder behavior

---

## Algorithm Summary

For each decimation round:

1. Run BP for `decimation_inner_iters`.
2. If syndrome satisfied → return immediately.
3. Select the unfrozen variable with maximal |posterior LLR|.
4. Tie-break by lowest variable index (deterministic).
5. Zero-posterior convention → freeze positive (hard = 0).
6. Clamp LLR to ±`decimation_freeze_llr`.
7. Repeat up to `decimation_rounds`.

If convergence fails:

Fallback candidate ranking (explicit and deterministic):

    (syndrome_weight, hamming_weight, round_index)

No randomness is introduced.

---

## Added Parameters  
(Validated only when `postprocess="guided_decimation"`)

- `decimation_rounds` (default: 10)
- `decimation_inner_iters` (default: 10)
- `decimation_freeze_llr` (default: 1000.0)

Baseline decoder calls ignore these parameters.

---

## Guarantees

- No changes to flooding or layered BP loops
- No changes to `_bp_postprocess()`
- No schema changes (`SCHEMA_VERSION` and `INTEROP_SCHEMA_VERSION` unchanged)
- No identity/hash drift for baseline decoders
- No new dependencies
- Fully deterministic across repeated runs

All existing tests pass without modification.  
31 new guided-decimation tests added.

---

## Out of Scope (Deferred)

The following structural techniques remain candidates for future releases:

- Stabilizer Inactivation
- MP-aware OSD
- Check reweighting
- Sequential CN scheduling
- Graph surgery
- Channel model changes
- Learned components
- Automatic schedule switching

---

## Backward Compatibility

Fully backward compatible.

Behavior is unchanged unless explicitly enabled via:

    postprocess="guided_decimation"

title: v3.3.1 — Geometry Diagnostics Hardening
body:
## v3.3.1 — Geometry Diagnostics Hardening

This release hardens the geometry diagnostics layer introduced in v3.3.0.  
No decoder behavior, schema version, or benchmark artifacts were modified.

### 🔒 Hardening Improvements

- **BSIConfigError introduced**
  - `compute_bsi` now raises a specific `BSIConfigError` (subclass of `ValueError`)
    when base/2x benchmark records are misaligned.
  - Prevents silent configuration mismatches.
  - Extra 2x-only records are explicitly documented as ignored.

- **SSI Aggregation Safety**
  - Confirmed grouping by `(decoder, distance, p)` to prevent cross-decoder mixing.

- **DPS Regression Precision**
  - Linear regression now fits full-precision `log10(FER)` values.
  - Rounding applied only at serialization.
  - Determinism preserved.

- **README Badge Verified**
  - Release badge aligned with v3.3.0 tag.

### ✅ Guarantees

- No changes to decoder core logic.
- No channel abstraction changes.
- No schema changes.
  - `SCHEMA_VERSION = 3.0.1`
  - `INTEROP_SCHEMA_VERSION = 3.1.2`
- No sidecar regeneration.
- No heavy simulations rerun.
- Deterministic outputs unchanged.
- 670 tests passed (7 skipped).

### Context

v3.3.0 introduced Tier A geometry-aware diagnostics instrumentation.  
v3.3.1 ensures that diagnostic aggregation and mismatch handling are strictly enforced and future-proofed.

This release completes the geometry diagnostics stabilization phase.

title: v3.3.0 — Geometry-Aware Syndrome-Only Diagnostics
body:
v3.3.0 introduces a diagnostics-first reporting layer to analyze
distance scaling inversion under bsc_syndrome inference in
deterministic QLDPC CSS decoding.

This release adds measurement instrumentation only.
No decoder behavior changes are included.

────────────────────────────────────────
New Diagnostics (Layer 3)
────────────────────────────────────────

• Distance Penalty Slope (DPS)
  Slope of log10(FER + eps) vs distance per (decoder, p).
  Positive slope indicates distance scaling inversion.

• False-Convergence Rate (FCR)
  P(syndrome = 0 AND logical failure),
  algebraically equivalent to SCR − Fidelity.

• Budget Sensitivity Index (BSI)
  FER(base_max_iters) − FER(2x_max_iters),
  quantifies iteration budget sensitivity.

• Schedule Sensitivity Index (SSI)
  max(FER) − min(FER) across schedules
  per (decoder, distance, p).

• Per-iteration summaries derived post-hoc from llr_history:
  - syndrome_weight[t]
  - check_satisfaction_ratio[t]
  - delta_syndrome[t]

• Aggregate stall metrics
• Residual summaries (energy, linf, l2 statistics)
• Local inconsistency summary (syndrome weight increase events)

All diagnostic outputs are emitted as deterministic sidecar artifacts.
Canonical benchmark outputs remain unchanged.

────────────────────────────────────────
Architectural Guarantees
────────────────────────────────────────

• No changes to decoder logic
• No scheduling modifications

title: v3.2.1 — Inversion Index Formalization & Cross-Channel Structural Diagnostic
body:
📦 Release Title

v3.2.1 — Inversion Index Formalization & Cross-Channel Structural Diagnostic

📝 Release Notes
Overview

v3.2.1 formalizes the Inversion Index (II) as a deterministic diagnostic metric and completes the structural comparison between the oracle and syndrome-only channel models.

This release introduces no decoder, channel, schema, or artifact modifications.
All benchmark data remains unchanged.
All determinism guarantees are preserved.

629 tests passed / 7 skipped.

What’s New
1️⃣ Inversion Index (II)

A derived diagnostic metric:

II = SCR - Fidelity
   = syndrome_consistency_rate - (1 - FER)

The Inversion Index isolates syndrome-consistent but logically incorrect decoding outcomes.

Interpretation:

II > 0 → decoder is producing structurally valid but logically wrong corrections

II = 0 → no systematic inversion mechanism

II = 1.0 → maximal inversion (oracle p > 0.50 regime)

The metric is an exact algebraic derivative of existing deterministic fields.
No new benchmark runs were required.

2️⃣ Structural Channel Comparison

This release completes the formal comparative analysis between:

Oracle channel (v3.1.4)

Syndrome-only channel (v3.2.0)

Key structural findings:

Property	Oracle	Syndrome-Only
Effective threshold	~0.50	~0.01–0.02
Inversion regime	Yes (p > 0.50)	None
Inversion Index peak	1.0	~0.0
SCR/FER divergence	Yes	No
Schedule differentiation	Masked	Real
Distance scaling	Positive	Negative

The Inversion Index is the clearest single metric separating the two models.

3️⃣ Statistical Noise Bound

Small non-zero II values observed under the syndrome-only channel are now formally bounded.

For a linear code with m independent parity checks:

P[random syndrome match] ≈ 2^(−m)
Expected matches ≈ T · 2^(−m)

Observed II values (~0.002–0.010) correspond to ~1–5 events per 500 trials, consistent with random structural coincidence — not a hidden inversion regime.

This closes the interpretive loop and strengthens peer defensibility.

Determinism & Governance

Layer 1 (decoder): unchanged

Channel logic: unchanged

Schema: 3.0.1 (unchanged)

Canonical JSON: unchanged

Artifact hashes: unchanged

No new dependencies

Runtime mode: deterministic

II is derived from existing metrics and inherits determinism guarantees.

Tests
629 passed, 7 skipped

No regressions.

Why This Release Matters

v3.2.1 marks the transition from:

“Benchmark reporting”

to

“Structural regime diagnosis.”

It establishes:

A scalar inversion diagnostic

A formal separation between channel artifacts and decoder behavior

A bounded explanation for stochastic effects

A stable comparative baseline for future decoder improvements

title: v3.1.3 — Syndrome-Only Inference (Realistic Channel Modeling)
body:
v3.1.3 introduces an explicit channel abstraction layer and adds
a realistic syndrome-only inference mode to eliminate the
degenerate 0.0 FER behavior observed under oracle LLR construction.

This release preserves full determinism and decoder immutability
while enabling scientifically meaningful threshold behavior.

Channel Abstraction
-------------------

New pluggable channel layer:

    channel_model = "oracle" | "bsc_syndrome"

• oracle (default)
  - Identical behavior to v3.1.2
  - Byte-identical deterministic artifacts
  - No schema changes
  - No decoder modifications

• bsc_syndrome
  - Uniform LLR: log((1 - p) / p)
  - No access to true error sign
  - Decoder infers purely from syndrome constraints
  - Produces non-zero FER at small p
  - Enables realistic threshold curves
  - Reveals schedule differentiation

• No scheduling or OSD changes

  431f7573a0ba8af4784b385f528cfe99d6169eb74798eabddd146def278b6d77

Validation Summary
------------------

• 629 tests passing (7 skipped)
• Oracle artifact identity confirmed
• BSC mode produces realistic FER curves
• Mean iterations > 1 under realistic noise
• Layered schedule shows measurable improvement over flooding

Scientific Impact
-----------------

v3.1.2 established deterministic benchmarking and interop
infrastructure.

v3.1.3 transitions QEC into scientifically realistic channel
modeling without altering decoding semantics.

The decoder is now stress-tested under syndrome-only conditions,
providing a meaningful foundation for future algorithmic
improvements.

Small is beautiful.

Stability is engineered.

title: v2.9.0 — Deterministic Adaptive Scheduling
body:
## v2.9.0 — Deterministic Adaptive Scheduling

This release introduces a strictly deterministic, one-way adaptive schedule controller for the belief-propagation decoder.

The goal of v2.9.0 is controlled iteration budgeting without introducing non-determinism or dynamic residual switching.

---

### ✨ Added

- `schedule="adaptive"`: two-phase deterministic controller
  - Phase 1: `flooding` for k1 iterations
  - Phase 2: `hybrid_residual` for remaining iterations
- Default `k1 = max(1, max_iters // 4)`
- Cumulative iteration accounting (public contract)
- Explicit validation of adaptive parameters
- Comprehensive test coverage for adaptive behavior

---

### 🔒 Determinism Guarantees

- Strictly one-way switching (no dynamic backtracking)
- No internal message state shared between phases
- Same inputs → same schedule path → same output
- No randomness introduced
- All existing schedules unchanged

---

### 🧪 Stability

- Full test suite passing
- No behavior changes to:
  - `flooding`
  - `layered`
  - `residual`
  - `hybrid_residual`
  - `ensemble_k`

---

### 📌 Design Constraints (Intentional)

- Adaptive scheduling is explicitly non-residual-dynamic in v2.9.0
- Ensemble decoding is not extended inside adaptive mode
- Logging remains minimal and opt-in

Future milestones may extend adaptive behavior, but v2.9.0 prioritizes determinism and architectural clarity.

---

**Summary**

v2.9.0 formalizes adaptive scheduling as a controlled, deterministic wrapper layer over existing schedules while preserving all prior guarantees.

title: v2.8.0 — Deterministic Scheduling, Ensemble & State-Aware Enhancements
body:
v2.8.0 — Deterministic Scheduling, Ensemble & State-Aware Enhancements

This release introduces deterministic scheduling refinements, dual-parameter min-sum variants, ensemble decoding, and state-aware residual weighting — all with full regression stability (339 passed / 7 skipped / 0 failed).

✨ improved_norm / improved_offset Modes

Extended min-sum variants with dual scaling parameters:

alpha1 applies to the first minimum

alpha2 applies to the second minimum

Both flooding and layered schedules updated consistently.
Backward-compatible with existing min-sum modes.

🔁 hybrid_residual Schedule

Deterministic hybrid of layered and residual scheduling:

Even/odd check-node partitioning

Within each layer: descending residual ordering

Optional hybrid_residual_threshold prioritizes high-residual checks

Strict deterministic tie-breaking by ascending check index

Validation scoped correctly to hybrid schedule only

🎯 Deterministic Ensemble Decoding (ensemble_k)

Adds reproducible multi-pass decoding without RNG:

Member 0 uses exact baseline LLR

Other members use deterministic zero-mean alternating perturbations

Selection priority:

Converged solution

Lowest syndrome weight

Lowest member index (deterministic tie-break)

Optimized to avoid repeated matrix casting inside ensemble loop

Fully deterministic across runs.

🧠 State-Aware Residual Weighting (state_aware_residual)

Residual ordering can now be modulated by per-check state parameters:

weight = s_by_state[label] * |cos(phi_by_state[label])|

Applied multiplicatively to raw residuals

Precomputed once per decode call (no per-iteration trig overhead)

Strict validation:

Integer labels

Non-negative

In-range

Length must equal number of checks

Disabled by default (zero behavioral change when off)

🛠️ Validation & Performance Improvements

Alpha parameter semantics aligned with documentation

Hybrid threshold validation scoped to hybrid schedule only

Ensemble syndrome computation optimized (H32 reuse)

State-aware weights precomputed once per call

Residual update block verified singular and deterministic

🧪 Regression Status

339 passed

7 skipped

0 failed

Determinism verified across repeated runs

CI green

Sourcery review resolved

Summary

v2.8.0 strengthens deterministic behavior, expands scheduling flexibility, improves performance, and adds state-aware modulation — without breaking existing workflows.

v2.7.0 — Deterministic Residual Scheduling

This release introduces an opt-in residual-based scheduling strategy for layered belief propagation while preserving full backward compatibility and deterministic execution guarantees.

✨ New Feature
Residual-Ordered Layered Scheduling (schedule="residual")

A new scheduling mode for bp_decode that reorders check nodes each iteration based on descending maximum message residual.

Residual defined as:
max |new_msg - old_msg| per check node

Deterministic tie-breaking via ascending check index

Stable lexicographic ordering using NumPy

No randomness introduced

Default behavior unchanged

This provides faster convergence on many instances while maintaining reproducibility and invariant safety.

🔒 Determinism & Compatibility

No changes to public API

Fully compatible with:

sum_product

min_sum

norm_min_sum

offset_min_sum

damping

clipping

LLR history tracking

OSD post-processing

Residual ordering is recomputed deterministically each iteration using stable sorting. Execution remains reproducible across runs.

12 new residual schedule tests added

312 total tests passing in project suite

Reduces iteration counts relative to flooding

title: v2.5.0 — Deterministic Statistical Rigor & Layered Decoding
body:
v2.5.0 — Deterministic Statistical Rigor & Layered Decoding

This release extends the QEC framework with opt-in statistical rigor and improved decoding efficiency while preserving full backward compatibility with v2.4.0.

All new features are disabled by default. Existing workflows remain bit-identical.

✨ New Features
1️⃣ Wilson Score Confidence Intervals (Opt-in)

Monte Carlo FER simulations can now compute continuity-corrected Wilson score confidence intervals.

ci_method="wilson"

Adjustable alpha

gamma >= 0 continuity correction (set gamma=0 to disable correction)

CI computation is deterministic and introduces no external dependencies.

2️⃣ Deterministic Early Termination

Simulations can stop early once CI width falls below a user-defined threshold:

early_stop_epsilon=<float>

The break condition is deterministic: same seed + same epsilon → identical termination point.

3️⃣ Layered (Serial) BP Scheduling

Added schedule="layered" to bp_decode().

Incremental LLR updates

O(nnz(H)) per iteration

Typically fewer iterations than flooding

Fully deterministic (fixed check-node order)

Flooding remains the default.

4️⃣ OSD-1 Post-Processing

Extended OSD-0 with a single least-reliable pivot bit flip:

postprocess="osd1"

Deterministic tie-breaking

Preserves never-degrade guarantee

🔒 Determinism & Backward Compatibility

No new randomness introduced.

No stochastic branching.

Default parameters reproduce v2.4.0 output exactly.

247/247 core tests passing.

🧮 Statistical & Numerical Improvements

Wilson CI uses integer-grounded frame error counts.

gamma >= 0 validation aligned with documented contract.

Validation scoped to CI-enabled runs only.

🚀 Summary

v2.5.0 strengthens the framework’s statistical foundations while introducing more efficient decoding options — without sacrificing determinism or stability.

title: QSOLKCB/QEC v2.4.0 — Multi-Mode BP Decoder, OSD-0 Recovery, Deterministic FER Harness
body:
QSOLKCB/QEC v2.4.0

This release introduces performance hardening, deterministic simulation infrastructure, and post-processing recovery for CSS QLDPC decoding — while preserving full backward compatibility.

✨ Highlights
Multi-Mode Belief Propagation

Uses dense GF(2) row-echelon reduction

No SciPy dependency

Deterministic FER Simulation Harness

src/simulation/fer.py


Provides:

Monte Carlo FER / BER estimation

title: v2.3.0 — Decoder Utility Formalization and Stability Refinement
body:
QSOLKCB/QEC v2.3.0 — Decoder Utility Layer Formalization and Stability Refinement
Release Notes
Overview

Version 2.3.0 formalizes the decoder layer into explicit, composable utilities while preserving full backward compatibility with the existing JointSPDecoder implementation. This release introduces a clear separation between detection, inference, correction, and channel modeling, and adds targeted validation and micro-optimizations for numerical robustness.

All changes are purely additive or refinement-level. No existing APIs were modified and no architectural behavior changed.

Added
Decoder Utility Layer (Additive)

A new standalone decoder utility section has been introduced:

update_pauli_frame(frame, correction)
Pure GF(2) XOR Pauli-frame update (non-mutating, validated).

syndrome(H, e)
Standalone binary syndrome computation mirroring syndrome_X / syndrome_Z.

bp_decode(H, llr, max_iter, syndrome_vec)
Standalone belief-propagation decoder operating on per-variable LLR vectors.

detect(H, e)
Thin wrapper over syndrome.

infer(H, llr, max_iter, syndrome_vec)
Thin wrapper over bp_decode.

channel_llr(e, p, bias)
Channel log-likelihood computation with optional scalar or per-variable bias weighting.

This establishes an explicit detection → inference → correction workflow independent of class-bound methods.

Refined
Input Validation

channel_llr now enforces p ∈ (0, 1) to prevent undefined or numerically unstable behavior at boundary values.

Micro-Optimization

bp_decode now precomputes:

H_int32

syndrome_uint8

eliminating repeated casting in the early-stopping condition.

No decoding logic was altered.

Testing

36 new unit tests added for decoder utilities.

Full test suite: 101 passed, 0 failed.

No regressions in existing construction, CSS orthogonality, or decoder tests.

Architectural Context

With v2.3.0:

Construction layer is algebraically guaranteed (v2.1.0).

Decoder layer is numerically stable under sparse edge cases (v2.2.0).

Decoder utilities are now modular, explicit, and test-covered (v2.3.0).

This release completes the formal separation of detection, inference, and correction logic while preserving deterministic behavior.

title: QSOLKCB/QEC v2.2.0 — Belief Propagation Stability Hardening for QLDPC CSS Codes
body:
Release Description — v2.2.0

Version 2.2.0 introduces a numerical stability correction to the belief-propagation (sum-product) decoder implemented in the JointSPDecoder class of the QSOLKCB/QEC framework. This release resolves the handling of degree-1 parity-check nodes in the Tanner graph, ensuring consistency with proper extrinsic belief-propagation semantics.

In prior versions, degree-1 checks implicitly fell through to the general tanh-product update rule:

L_{c->v} = 2 * atanh( Π_{v' ≠ v} tanh( L_{v'->c} / 2 ) )


For a single-neighbor check node, the product is taken over an empty set.
By definition, the empty product equals 1. This leads to:

atanh(1) → ∞


Under finite-precision arithmetic, this produces extremely large log-likelihood values, injecting artificial confidence into the decoding process and effectively double-counting intrinsic channel information.

Under finite-precision arithmetic, this injected artificial log-likelihood amplification into the decoding process, effectively double-counting intrinsic channel evidence and potentially destabilizing sparse or irregular LDPC structures.

The updated implementation explicitly sets the outgoing check-to-variable message to 0.0 for degree-1 parity checks, enforcing zero extrinsic contribution and restoring mathematically correct BP behavior. This modification:

Eliminates artificial confidence injection,

Improves numerical robustness for sparse Tanner graphs,

Preserves convergence behavior under low-noise regimes,

Maintains deterministic decoder operation.

No changes were made to the construction layer, additive lift invariants (v2.1.0), CSS orthogonality guarantees, finite-field lifting framework, or rank computation logic. All existing regression tests pass unchanged.

This release constitutes a decoder stability hardening increment with no architectural modifications to the QLDPC CSS construction framework.

title: v2.1.0 — Invariant-Hardened CSS Construction
body:
# v2.1.0 — Deterministic Additive Lift & Invariant Hardening

This release completes the invariant-hardening phase introduced after v2.0.0.

## 🔁 Lift Redesign (Major Internal Upgrade)

Replaced per-edge random lift tables with deterministic additive shifts:

    s(i,j) = (row_shift[i] + col_shift[j]) mod L

Properties:
- No per-edge RNG dictionary
- No cached lookup table
- Deterministic across processes
- Order-independent
- Algebraically preserves CSS orthogonality

## 🧮 Orthogonality Guarantee

For circulant matrices A, B:

    B_X = [ A | B ]
    B_Z = [ Bᵀ | Aᵀ ]

Since circulants commute:

    AB = BA

Therefore:

    B_X @ B_Zᵀ = AB + BA = 2AB ≡ 0 (mod 2)

Lifted orthogonality is preserved via the additive shift invariant.

No repair logic. No retry loops. Pure algebra.

## 🧪 Test Suite Status

- 89 tests collected
- 89 passed
- 0 failed

Expanded coverage includes:
- Lift-table determinism
- CSS orthogonality across seeds/sizes
- Circulant block validation
- GF(2^e) arithmetic correctness
- Binary rank validation
- Negative orthogonality break tests
- Backend factory coverage

## 🧠 Design Philosophy

- Deterministic > stochastic
- Invariants > heuristics
- Sparse > dense
- Fail fast > silent corruption

This marks the formal stabilization of the lifted CSS construction framework.

title: QEC v2.0.0 — QLDPC protograph CSS codes
body:
QEC v2.0.0 — QLDPC Protograph CSS Codes & Structural Invariants

Release date: 2026
Type: Major release (architectural upgrade)

Summary

QEC v2.0.0 introduces a modern quantum LDPC (QLDPC) subsystem based on protograph-constructed CSS codes over finite fields. This release upgrades core correctness guarantees by enforcing CSS orthogonality by construction, adds deterministic code generation, and expands the test suite to encode theoretical invariants directly.

Existing qutrit Golay and ququart lattice-based stabilizer components remain fully supported and unchanged in behavior.

Major Additions

Protograph-based QLDPC CSS codes (NEW)

Orthogonal protograph pair construction over GF(2^e)

Shared circulant lifting per protograph edge

CSS condition H_X · H_Zᵀ = 0 enforced structurally (no post-hoc repair)

Column-weight-2 LDPC ensembles

Deterministic, seeded construction

Binary expansion via finite-field lifting

Predefined code rates: 0.50, 0.60, 0.75

Joint X/Z Sum-Product Decoder

Explicit handling of correlated X/Z errors under depolarizing noise

Compatible with protograph-based QLDPC ensembles

Expanded Simulation & Benchmarking

Frame-error-rate (FER) simulation utilities

Hashing bound implemented as an asymptotic reference, not a finite-length guarantee

Trend-based threshold tests rather than fixed numeric claims

Correctness & Invariants

CSS orthogonality enforced at construction time

Removal of iterative “repair” logic for parity-check matrices

Hard invariant checks with explicit failure modes

Deterministic reproducibility for all predefined ensembles

Test Suite Expansion

65 new QLDPC-specific tests

GF(2^e) arithmetic validation

Protograph orthogonality verification

Column-weight and structural invariants

Decoder sanity and simulation tests

All predefined codes validated

97 total tests, all passing

Existing Systems (Unchanged)

Ternary Golay qutrit code [[11,1,5]]₃

Ququart stabilizer code (d = 4)

D₄ lattice geometric prior for ququart decoding

Legacy simulation and visualization tooling

Versioning Notes

v2.0.0 represents a semantic and architectural upgrade over v1.x

Core construction guarantees and testing philosophy have changed

Internal APIs for QLDPC construction are new and may evolve

v1.6 remains valid for historical Golay/ququart experiments

Citation

If you use this release in academic or technical work, please cite:

Concept DOI (latest):
https://doi.org/10.5281/zenodo.17742258

A version-specific DOI for v2.0.0 will be minted via Zenodo after release publication.

Acknowledgements

This release incorporates recent advances in protograph-based QLDPC code construction (Komoto–Kasai, 2025) and integrates them with QEC’s existing non-binary stabilizer and geometric decoding framework.

title: QEC v1.6 — Ququart + Qutrit Golay + Geometry Layer
body:

alongside the existing ququart + D₄ lattice geometry framework.

New in v1.6:

title: QEC v1.5 — Ququart Stabilizer Code + High-Density Geometry Layer
body:
# QEC v1.5 — Ququart Stabilizer Code + High-Density Geometry Layer

This release expands the QSOL-IMC QEC stack into higher-dimensional
quantum systems with the introduction of:

1. A **[[3,1]]₄ ququart stabilizer code** (d = 4 qudit)
2. A **fully generic stabilizer engine** for arbitrary dimension d
3. A new **Layer 2: High-Density Geometry Prior** (D₄ / E8-inspired)
4. A **ququart threshold-style Monte Carlo simulator**
5. A complete **geometry-augmented threshold benchmark**

This update integrates higher-dimensional qudit logic, generalized Pauli
operators, lattice priors, and stabilizer decoding into a unified,
robust simulation environment compatible with the QSOL-IMC research
program.
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

## 🔧 New Modules

### • `src/qec_ququart.py`
Full implementation of a [[3,1]]₄ ququart stabilizer code:
- Uses generalized Pauli operators X₄, Z₄
- Stabilizers S₁ = Z₁ Z₂^{-1}, S₂ = Z₂ Z₃^{-1}
- Logical operators X_L = X₁ X₂ X₃, Z_L = Z₁
- Syndrome extraction in ℤ₄
- Decoding of single-ququart X^±1 errors

### • `src/qudit_stabilizer.py`
A reusable, dimension-agnostic stabilizer engine:
- Arbitrary dimension d
- Generalized X_d, Z_d construction
- Multi-qudit Pauli building from ℤ_d exponent vectors
- Expectation + syndrome extraction
- Forms the foundation for qutrit, ququart, and higher qudit codes

### • `src/ququart_lattice_prior.py`
The new **Geometry Layer** for ququart codes:
- Works in 4D logical amplitude space
- Projects onto:
    - square lattice Z⁴ (baseline)
    - high-density D₄ lattice (E8-inspired surrogate)
- Supports sharpening via beta parameter
- Rebuilds denoised logical states before stabilizer decoding
- Implements the QSOL concept of **geometry as a pre-decoder**

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

## 📊 Threshold Experiments

### • `examples/ququart_threshold_demo.py`
Threshold-style Monte Carlo simulation for the [[3,1]]₄ code.

### • `examples/ququart_threshold_with_prior.py`
Same experiment, but with geometric priors applied.

Results:
- Logical error rate p_log is consistently reduced at all p
- D₄ lattice priors act as a **noise-compressing stabilizer surface**
- The geometry layer yields a measurable **upward shift in threshold**
- Empirical confirmation that structured geometry improves QEC robustness

Output:
`/tmp/ququart_threshold.png`  
`/tmp/ququart_lattice_prior_threshold.png`

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

## 🧠 Conceptual Impact

This release formalizes **the first full higher-dimensional (d > 3) component**
in the QSOL-IMC QEC ecosystem.

It establishes:
- Compatibility of ququart systems with the QSOL stance + geometry layers
- A demonstration of how higher-dimensional qudits naturally integrate with
  dense lattice priors (D₄, E8)
- A new geometric pre-decoding mechanism that can be generalized across d

This is a crucial milestone in the QSOL-IMC philosophy of:
> “latent geometry as information protection”

and creates a fertile ground for:
- Λ₂₄ / Leech lattice extensions  
- SU(4)-based stance operators  
- Unified qudit QEC architectures  
- Hybrid geometry + stabilizer decoding research  

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

## 📁 Added Files

title: QEC v1.4 — High-Density Lattice Priors + Threshold Projection Decoder
body:
# QEC v1.4 — Lattice-Stabilized Threshold Upgrade

This release introduces the **High-Density Lattice Geometry Layer**, a 
new module that implements geometric projection onto high-density lattices 
(square baseline and hexagonal / E8-surrogate) prior to QEC decoding.

When integrated into the Steane [[7,1,3]] code, the lattice projection 
acts as a **geometric pre-decoder** that suppresses small-amplitude noise 
and improves the effective logical error rate across the physical error 
range.

This is the first QSOL-IMC demonstration that:

- **Geometry alone can shift QEC thresholds upward**
- High-density lattices behave like *implicit stabilizer surfaces*
- Projection onto a dense Voronoi domain is equivalent to 
  nearest-codeword decoding in coding theory
- E8-class geometry provides a tangible performance advantage

---

## 🔧 New Features

### • Lattice Geometry Layer (`high_density_lattice.py`)
Implements:
- Square projection (Zⁿ baseline)
- Hexagonal projection (2D E8 surrogate)
- Vectorized projection for state vectors and density matrices
- Pre-decoding hooks for QEC simulations

### • Extended Threshold Simulation
`LatticeThresholdSimulation` wraps existing ThresholdSimulation to apply 
geometric projection before Steane decoding.

### • Demonstration Script
`examples/qec_threshold_with_lattice_priors.py`  
Provides a fully reproducible experiment showing:

- Baseline Steane threshold curve
- Lattice-augmented threshold curve
- Improvement visualization (saved to `/tmp/qec_lattice_threshold.png`)

### • Unit Tests
`tests/test_high_density_lattice.py`  
Validates:
- Minimal-distance superiority of high-density lattices
- Error-reducing properties under noise

---

## 📊 Scientific Impact

This release formalizes a key concept of the **QSOL Unified Field Geometry Layer**:

> High-density lattice priors convert latent geometry into a stabilizing 
> error-correcting scaffold, offering QEC-like robustness without needing 
> explicit quantum circuitry.

This integrates seamlessly with:
- SU(3)/qutrit stance operators (Layer 1)
- E8 triality and Sierpinski–Rubik multiscale geometry
- QSOL-IMC memetic reactor models

---

## 📁 Added Files
